responseSet.title: In the Shadow of Moloch: The Sacrifice of Children and its Impact on Western Religions.: By Martin S. Bergmann.  New York:  Columbia University Press. Pp. 347.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In the Shadow of Moloch: The Sacrifice of Children and its Impact on Western Religions.

By Martin S. Bergmann. New York: Columbia University Press. Pp. 347.

Jerome Oremland

Jerome Oremland

Jerome D. Oremland
1134 Lake Street, San Francisco, California 94118

Jerome Oremland  

Many generations of psychoanalysts and students of the humanities will enjoy and learn from Martin S. Bergmann's remarkable volume In the Shadow of Moloch. Those of us who know his writings, seminars, and lively informal discussions are not surprised by the breadth of scholarly knowledge that Bergmann brings to bear on a subject rarely addressed by psychoanalysts, the understanding of 'the religious past of western culture'. The book's eleven pages of bibliography are in themselves an enticing array of psychoanalysis, theology, philosophy, history, literature, and anthropology. Although the bibliography is richly laced with modern scholarship, many of the titles and the authors remind us of that shining past when psychoanalysis richly interrelated with the classics and the humanities. While in no way dated, in tone and style the book reminds us of the salad days of psychoanalysis when psychoanalysts dared to study broadly the characteristics that define the human being.

The book explicates the role of ritualised slaughter, particularly of children, in the development of humankind's moral sense and defining of values. The title, In the Shadow of Moloch, reveals the complexity of Bergmann's approach. Shadow, in Bergmann's sophisticated discussion, takes on the Jungian sense of the 'dark side', an internal that is integral.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Memory in Mind and Brain

By Morton F. Reiser. New York: Basic Books. 1990. Pp. 218.

Eva Lester

Eva Lester

Eva P. Lester
1025 Pine Ave West, Montreal, H3A 1A1

Eva Lester  

This is an ambitious book, far-reaching in its scope. Reiser takes up the task that Freud abandoned when he set aside the Project for a Scientific Psychology and turned to the 'figurative language peculiar to psychology' in order to understand the complex observations he was making while listening to his patients. This is not Reiser's first effort in this direction. In 1984 he published Mind, Brain, Body: Towards a Convergence of Psychoanalysis and Neurobiology, his first major step to test 'biology's unlimited possibilities' (Freud, 1920, p. 60) to answer the questions we put to it.

Reiser is particularly suited to take up a similar task.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

responseSet.title: Bright Air, Brilliant Fire: On the Matter of the Mind; A Nobel Laureate's Revolutionary Vision of How the Mind Originates in the Brain: By Gerald M. Edelman.  New York:  Basic Books. 1992.  Pp. 280.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bright Air, Brilliant Fire: On the Matter of the Mind; A Nobel Laureate's Revolutionary Vision of How the Mind Originates in the Brain

By Gerald M. Edelman. New York: Basic Books. 1992. Pp. 280.

Lawrence Kunstadt

Lawrence Kunstadt

Lawrence P. Kunstadt
The Psychoanalytic Institute, New York University
401 East 34th St, NY 10016

Lawrence Kunstadt  

Is this a dagger which I see before me,

The handle toward mine hand? Come, let me clutch thee.

I have thee not, and yet I see thee still.

Art thou not, fatal vision, sensible

To feeling as to sight? or art thou but

A dagger of the mind, a false creation?

Proceeding from the heat-oppressed brain?

Macbeth

Incited by his wife to murder gentle King Duncan, inflamed by her affronts to his manhood and racked with guilt, Macbeth hallucinates the weapon of death. Therein lies a telling difference between psychoanalysis and neurobiology. For no matter how well we come to know the brain, can our knowledge ever account for the regicidal, and presumably parricidal, wish and its participation in intrapsychic conflicts?

In contrast to neurobiology, which is an inquiry into mechanism, psychoanalysis is at minimum a psychology of meaning, especially personal meaning. So the question is, while neurobiology cannot account for meaning, can the mind therefore be understood without recourse to biology? Edelman argues that meaning outside of Darwinian evolution and epigenetic development is incomplete. The brain being the organ of meaning, understanding mechanisms of brain functioning are necessary to understand the mind. This is not an obvious matter, even for people who try to understand the brain/mind problem.

In a fundamental, critical and extended attack, Edelman undermines current functionalist (that the brain operates on formal logical algorithms), objectivist (that the brain accurately and in a one-to-one correspondence, reflects relations and categories of extemal reality), and, particularly, 'brain-as-computer' theories—which, in a certain way, are all wayward sons of Le Mettrie's 1747 view of animals and people as mechanical machines. (He later changed his mind.)

responseSet.title: The Interpretation of the Flesh: Freud and Femininity: By Teresa Brennan.  London:  Routledge.  1992.  Pp. 266.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Interpretation of the Flesh: Freud and Femininity

By Teresa Brennan. London: Routledge. 1992. Pp. 266.

Janet Sayers

Janet Sayers

Janet Sayers
Keynes College, University of Kent, Canterbury
Kent CT2 7NP

Janet Sayers  

Time was when the woman question was very productive within psychoanalysis. Not so today—all too often clinical practice ignores it. Meanwhile its treatment within feminist psychoanalytic theory has tended to be strait-jacketed by Lacanian orthodoxy.

Teresa Brennan's therapy-based introduction to this question and her non-Lacanian theorising are therefore refreshing. She starts with the observation that while women in analysis complain of feeling exhausted and depleted of energy, men's preoccupations are more verbal. It is this gender difference that she seeks to explain. Specifically, she is concerned to resolve Freud's 'riddle of femininity' which, rather idiosyncratically, she equates with the question of why certain drives and affects are turned so disablingly against the self. Its urgency for Freud, she goes on, arose from the occurrence of this untoward feminine trait in men.

The vigour with which the early psychoanalysts debated this issue was hamstrung, Brennan continues, by their overlooking of the quantitative factor involved.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

responseSet.title: Charles Ives: 'My Father's Song': By Stuart Feder.  New Haven:  Yale University Press.  1992.  Pp. 396.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Charles Ives: 'My Father's Song'

By Stuart Feder. New Haven: Yale University Press. 1992. Pp. 396.

Michael Miller

Michael Miller

Michael Craig Miller
Dept. of Psychiatry, Beth Israel Hospital
330 Brookline Avenue, Boston, Massachusetts 02215

Michael Miller  

A psychoanalytic biography may be more useful for the questions it asks than for the answers it provides. Stuart Feder, by drawing attention to Charles Ives's boyhood—and in particular to his close yet conflicted

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

864

relationship with his father George—has made a major contribution to our understanding of the first great and quintessentially American composer.

What is innovative about this biography is its being two biographies in one. Dr Feder devotes the first quarter of the book to George Ives and makes a good case for how the father figured continuously in both the musical and non-musical life of the son. George had immense talent and was the explorer who stretched Charles's ears and forever represented the musical ideal in Charles's mind. An ideal and idealised figure, George also had human problems, and Feder outlines them exhaustively. Thus, we come to entertain the possibility of an unconscious ambivalence behind Charles's idolatry. George Ives was a lively and inventive band leader, a leader of men, a capable master of several intruments. Feder points out, however, that in nineteenth-century small-town America, being a musician was not a source of pride for a man and George's inability to provide adequately for his family forced him to rely—perhaps to his shame—on the mercantile prosperity of his older brothers. George's last years were probably bitter, his life without status in a provincial, small New England town.

Charles's feeling for his father was further complicated by George's sudden death during Charles's first year at Yale. The timing of his father's death, at the height of Charles's adolescent rebellion, may have exaggerated the tendency to memorialise George. Feder scrutinises Charles Ives's nostalgia and points out Ives's attachment to the nineteenth-century world of his father, despite living the majority of his life in the twentieth.

Though Ives's music was revolutionary, he was decidedly not avant-garde. The spectacularly fascinating mixture of sweet nostalgia and adventurous musical experimentation is an evocation of his father's musical values and represents both his own and his father's passion for strong ears, capable of wrestling with sounds as they exist in the world, and disdain for a music 'gussied up' according to an intellectual, European parochialism. Yet Ives—with formal training from his father and from Horatio Parker at Yale—was no musical naïf: he was an artist with a great command of compositional tools.

responseSet.title: From the Words of my Mouth: Tradition in Psychotherapy: Edited by L. Spurling.  London:  Tavistock.  1993.  Pp. 165.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From the Words of my Mouth: Tradition in Psychotherapy

Edited by L. Spurling. London: Tavistock. 1993. Pp. 165.

Stella Pierides

Stella Pierides

Stella Pierides
5 Roseberry Gardens, London N4 1JQ

Stella Pierides  

The word tradition is said to come from the Latin tradere, meaning both to hand over or deliver, and to betray. Spurling sets the scene for this collection of papers by pointing to the ambivalent usage of the word. It has come to denote, on the one hand, something providing continuity without which there can be no progress, and, on the other, an attitude of unthinking attachment to a practice or form of knowledge which stands in the way of progress if not torn down.

Views on tradition are presented as standing between these two poles and the reader is given various questions as guides: What is a tradition? Are there different kinds, some of which are more open to revision and criticism than others? How do they come into being and how are they transmitted? And, most importantly, as psychotherapists, how do we come to find our own unique voice in our profession, and in whose name do we speak? Spurling goes on to discuss tradition as authority, considering the setting up of the psychoanalytic tradition and the means of passing it on and safeguarding it (though little is said about its continuous development). This leads him to warn that 'fascination' with our theories can be as stultifying as the illnesses our patients are trying to free themselves from, putting the issue in terms of the extremes of either a blind fascination with received theory or liberation from it.

The individual authors approach tradition with these questions in mind and create in their own way an interesting and thought-provoking book. There is some repetition and, perhaps unavoidably, given the nature of the enterprise, more breadth than depth.

The issue of 'Tradition in Training' is thoughtfully approached in Ellen Noonan's description of a training for student counsellors which uses psychoanalytical ideas to underpin and inform the forging of the professional identity of the trainee. She describes how a tradition in training has evolved which has space for questioning and revision and which is said to facilitate development in a way similar to the progression from adolescence to adulthood. The trainee counsellors proceed through the course to find their own voice through a variety of 'rebellious gestures'.

responseSet.title: Sigmund Freud–Ludwig Binswanger, Briefwechsel 1908-1938: Edited by Gerhard Fichtner.  Frankfurt am Main:  S. Fischer.  1992.  Pp. 340.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sigmund Freud–Ludwig Binswanger, Briefwechsel 1908-1938

Edited by Gerhard Fichtner. Frankfurt am Main: S. Fischer. 1992. Pp. 340.

Ludwig Haesler

Ludwig Haesler

Ludwig Haesler
Sulzbacher Weg 5, 6238 Hofheim am Taunus, Germany

Ludwig Haesler  

The German publishing house of S. Fischer has for some years now been issuing volumes of correspondence between Sigmund Freud and his contemporaries, and last year it was the turn of that between Sigmund Freud and the Swiss psychiatrist Ludwig Binswanger, published for the first time in an outstandingly presented edition by Gerhard Fichtner. The letters concerned were exchanged between two far from equal partners: Sigmund Freud, who had already crossed the threshold of the sixth decade of his life and was therefore concerned not only to safeguard the theory of psychoanalysis and ensure its further development, but also to consolidate its position in relation to other disciplines, in particular psychiatry, in the face of persistent, usually unobjective and in some cases malicious resistance; and Ludwig Binswanger, 25 years his junior, who had only just begun to familiarise himself with contemporary psychiatry during the course of his practical traineeship in medicine.

However, age was not the only major difference between the two correspondents. Unlike Freud, Ludwig Binswanger was a scion of a prominent Swiss family of psychiatrists with an upper middle-class background. His grandfather had founded the internationally renowned Bellevue Sanatorium at Kreuzlingen, near Konstanz, on Lake Constance; his father had become its medical director; and once Ludwig had completed his medical studies and psychiatric training, and obtained some practical experience, including a period with his uncle, Otto Binswanger, Professor of Psychiatry at Jena, and, in particular, a spell at Zürich's Burghölzli under Eugen Bleuler, he was to follow in his predecessors' footsteps as medical director of this sanatorium; because of his father's early death, however, he found himself at the helm of this institution much earlier than planned.

During his time at the Burghölzli in Zürich, the University Psychiatric Clinic headed by Bleuler, he met not only C. G. Jung, who was then senior physician and who supervised Ludwig Binswanger's dissertation on studies in association, but also, among others, Karl Abraham, Max Eitingon, Hermann Nunberg and A. A. Brill. As a result of their work in Zürich, in particular, these men had just begun to take an interest in psychoanalysis, and were subsequently to belong to the group of Freud's closest and most intimate pupils and followers.

[[RunningHead]]

From Fetus to Child

By Alessandra Piontelli. London and New York: Routledge and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis. 1992. Pp. 260.

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell

Judith Trowell
45 Heath Drive, Potter's Bar, Herts EN6 1EJ

Judith Trowell  

The field of observation has expanded and developed dramatically since Esther Bick's 1964 paper. Infant and young child observation was developed as a training tool for those wishing to embark on psychoanalytic training and then work with children. It soon became clear that observation skills, the capacity to observe the subject, the context, and one's own reactions to these observations, were an excellent introduction to any psychoanalytically-orientated training. Observation therefore became part of many trainings, and over time it began to emerge that the way in which observations were carried out and reported in the accompanying seminars could be predictive of a candidate's potential as an analyst or child therapist. This predictive aspect then spread to the observation itself. Could the information and understanding gained from observation be predictive in relation to the subject? By now observation was being used in a range of settings, not only with parents and babies or young children, but also adolescents, school children, children and adults with disability, those with learning difficulties and the elderly.

The possibility of looking retrospectively at observation material to see if there had been clues to the subsequent development seemed exciting. The analytic work of reconstruction or re-experiencing could be compared with children's and adults' actual experience, and by then some children and young people in analysis had been observational subjects as babies and young children. Could there, would there, be any links? In this book, Dr Piontelli has taken what seems like an obvious next step—prospective observation.

responseSet.title: Response to Maya Slater's Review of H. C. Halberstadt-Freud: Freud, Proust, Perversion and Love
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Maya Slater's Review of H. C. Halberstadt-Freud: Freud, Proust, Perversion and Love

Maya Slater

Maya Slater

Maya Slater
Queen Mary
Westfield College
Mile End Road
London E1 4NS

Maya Slater  

Maya Slater writes:

I readily accept that a novel such as Proust's can and does provide the analyst with fascinating insights not only into the mind of the characters, but also into that of the author. But when a work of fiction such as Proust's 'introspective' novel presents us with the illusion that the writer and the main character are one and the same, it is vital to remember that this is a narrative convention and not an absolute truth, and to acknowledge that this is so. Proust's personality differs from that of the hero of his novel in a huge number of important respects. To name only a few, the fictional character is a heterosexual, womanising Catholic with no sense of vocation, as different as possible from the homosexual, half-Jewish Proust, who was committed to a writing career from his teens. Proust warned us of this, describing his hero as 'a character whom I call "I", and who is not me'. This statement should surely be taken into consideration when the novel is scrutinised for its revelations.

September 1993

1270

responseSet.title: Psychoanalytic Case Studies: Edited by G. Pirooz Sholevar, with Jules Glenn.  Connecticut:  International Universities Press. 1991.  Pp. 286.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Case Studies

Edited by G. Pirooz Sholevar, with Jules Glenn. Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1991. Pp. 286.

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Albrecht Schwaber
4 Welland Road, Brookline, MA 02146

Evelyne Schwaber  

Four case studies are presented in this volume, carefully selected by the editors for their readability, and their ability to convey with clarity, and eloquence, the evolution and development of an analytic process. Considerable detail is offered to enhance understanding and identification with the process, but, as the editors indicate, they have attempted to strike a balance so as not to overwhelm the reader with too much detail. Two of these clinical studies are considered classics in the field, one by Rudolph Loewenstein—the analysis of a patient treated in the 1930s and first published in 1956—and the other by Leo Rangell, originally published in 1952. The other two are more recent analyses—one by Irwin Rosen, and a child analytic case described by Calvin Colarusso—both newly presented here. Each case, with an introduction and summary by the editors, is, in turn, discussed by two prominent analysts. This makes quite interesting reading.

The effort of the editors is commendable. As they write in their preface, 'This book was planned to satisfy the crying need for clinical data detailing the course of analysis from beginning to end. Only with such data can the serious scholar or the careful clinician get an accurate sense of what comprises an analysis. The data will enable the reader to evaluate the intermeshing work of patient and analyst and the mode of therapeutic action of a successful psychoanalysis' (p. 1). The ensuing responses to each case presented are 'intended to call attention to the new thoughts the cases would force upon the reader, current theory, technical engagements, and even errors and how they had affected the treatments. The discussions were to be freewheeling and unrestricted' (p. 3).

Guided by the high standards established by Freud's case reports, this undertaking is clearly intended to provide a rich source of clinical data, with the addition of differing perspectives on it. Leo Rangell further points out in his own introductory remarks, 'Whatever theoretical considerations stem from (these reports) would … be secondary to the data rather than the reverse. Validation and conviction would be more solid' (p. 5). Data is not to pre-empt theory. Such a venture is vital for the growth of our field and the editors are to be congratulated for their well-articulated effort in pursuing it.

responseSet.title: To be or not to be a Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To be or not to be a Patient

Jorgen Torp

Jorgen Torp

Jorgen Torp
Finsenvek 11A
DK-2000 Frederiksberg
Denmark

Jorgen Torp  

Dear Sirs,

It is often a nice quality to be able to be patient. It is not so nice when as a psychoanalyst you use the word in the medical sense, and thus accuse your analysand of being a patient although you know that your 'patient'

July 1992

180

is conscious of having a more/less serious neurotic problem/condition.

At the University of Copenhagen (February 1992) a lecture was given by Mme Janice de Saussure: 'Some basic psychoanalytic concepts'.

I liked the lecture, and I very much liked the way Mme Janice de Saussure presented herself and her lecture. My one objection to the lecture was, and still is, that repeatedly during the lecture the medical term patient was used, even though it was obvious that the case-histories in question were not derived from persons needing psychiatric attention at a hospital.

After the lecture given by Mme Janice de Saussure I objected to the use of the medical term patient, as to my mind it can reflect a serious flaw in the attitude of a psychoanalyst. Mme de Saussure said that it might very well be just a linguistic/cultural tradition.

I hope that Mme Janice de Saussure is right. I should like to think so.

I also like to read The International Review of Psycho-Analysis, but I do not feel confident that I can always take it that all authors understand the difference between an analysand and a patient.

I think that we owe our analysands and ourselves not to use the term patient, a term from 'Die Welt von Gestern' and long since obsolete. Using the term patient violates one of the more important basic psychoanalytic concepts and indicates that the psychoanalyst using it must still have a mental problem/flaw, which needs more attention on the couch.

As psychoanalysts we ought to know better than to see our analysands as patients. If we use the term patient we are ourself the patient. Therefore, as long as a psychoanalyst sticks to the term patient, I think that the psychoanalyst should refrain from practising as a psychoanalyst.

I should very much like to suggest that an analysand should never be seen as a patient, and I hope that shortly all authors will correct themselves, should they—tradition or not—tend to forget what Ella Freeman Sharpe, for example, wrote in her: 'Talks to students of psychoanalysis' (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., Volume XVIII, 1947).

responseSet.title: To be or not to be a Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

To be or not to be a Patient

Janice De Saussure

Janice De Saussure

Janice De Saussure  

Mme de Saussure writes:

Thank you for your letter informing me of the comment Mr Torp made about my use of the word patient in my talk given at the University of Copenhagen last Winter. As he told you, he made the same point during the discussion there. I can only say, as I did at the time, that for me what counts most is the basic attitude of respect the analyst has for the person in analysis. I do not think that one can jump to the conclusion that use of the word patient is adequate proof that the analyst looks down on or belittles the person in analysis nor that using the word analysand or client necessarily means respect. I did point out that the word chosen is related to the customs and usage in different parts of the world. Undoubtedly the meaning attributed to each term used to designate a person in analysis varies according to the point of view of the person using it or the one hearing it.

November 1992

181

responseSet.title: The Playground of Psychoanalytic Therapy: By Jean Sanville.  Hillsdale, NJ:  The Analytic Press. 1991.  Pp. 288.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Playground of Psychoanalytic Therapy

By Jean Sanville. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1991. Pp. 288.

Bhaskar Sripada

Bhaskar Sripada

Bhaskar Sripada
180 N. Michigan Avenue—Suite 540
Chicago, Illinois 60601

Bhaskar Sripada  

Jean Sanville's book, The Playground of Psychoanalytic Therapy, is an exploration of play and its clinical significance for the psychoanalytic psychotherapy of children and adults. Sanville traces her view of play to Freud's idea of transference as a playground. Because of the importance she attaches to the ideas of Winnicott and Balint, Sanville elaborates on the relational significance of play and attempts to integrate into this effort the rich findings from the infant-developmental research of Stern, Emde and Sander, etc. Her work also touches on the meaning of play for interpretation, transference, countertransference, dreams, personal mythology and termination. Sanville also explores the concept of play as drama. Her work is studded with many clinical examples exemplifying the implications of her theory for clinical practice. A few will be summarised and discussed.

Freud (1914) wrote, 'The main instrument, however, for curbing the patient's compulsion to repeat and for turning it into a motive for remembering lies in the handling of the transference. We render the compulsion harmless, and indeed useful, by giving it the right to assert itself in a definite field. We admit it into the transference as playground, in which it is allowed to expand in almost complete freedom and in which it is expected to is hidden in the patient's mind' (Remembering, repeating and working through, S.E. 12). Sanville (p. 86) bases her views on this idea of Freud's; however, she rejects Freud's emphasis on pathogenic instincts which she believes render the playground 'unsafe'. Because of this difference, Sanville defines the playground to be the potentially playful psychic space between the two participants in the dialogue or psychotherapy. Sanville's elaboration of play draws upon Winnicott's (1971) idea that, 'Psychotherapy takes place in the overlap of two areas of playing, that of the patient and that of the therapist. Psychotherapy has to do with two people playing together. The corollary of this is that where playing is not possible then the work done by the therapist is directed toward bringing the patient from a state of not being able to play' (Playing and Reality, New York: Basic Books, 1971, p. 38).

Cultural Theory and Psychoanalytic Tradition

By David James Fisher. New Brunswick and London: Transaction Publishers. 1991. Pp. 276.

George Pollock

George Pollock

George H. Pollock
5759 South Dorchester Avenue, Chicago, Illinois 60637-1726

George Pollock  

These are hard times for psychoanalysis in places where this field of study and clinical applications once flourished. As Ryan has noted in another context: 'Most American universities face a financially troubled future; public universities are affected by the tax problems of the states that finance them, while private universities find that … their usual sources of research funds all have shallower pockets in the current recession' ('Some cause for anxiety', Times Literary Supplement, 10 January 1992, p. 22).

Health care, a high priority in the United States, is due for a major change. Outcome studies, economic targets and goals, and managed care are but a few of the major challenges that impact upon the practice of psychoanalytic treatment, the recruitment of candidates for education, and the availability of patients who can afford psychoanalytic therapy. The excitement and enthusiasm that drove students and patients into psychoanalysis almost half a century ago are now much diminished. Newer 'cost-effective' treatments, an ever increasing number of 'spin-off' theories and approaches confuse the 'public', especially when new or innovative ideas are advanced and then 'attacked or demeaned' by fellow psychoanalysts.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

[[RunningHead]]

The Intimate Edge: Extending the Reach of Psychoanalytic Interaction

By Darlene Bregman Ehrenberg. New York and London: W. W. Norton & Company. 1992. Pp. 210.

L. Levi

L. Levi

L. David Levi
2112 F Street, N.W., #502, Washington, D.C. 20037

L. Levi  

In this compact volume Darlene Ehrenberg argues persuasively for focusing on the experience of the interaction between analyst and analysand to facilitate a genuinely effective analytic process. Initially, she recognises that technique, or what the analyst consciously intends, does not account for therapeutic action. She looks to the patient's reactions, especially the subtle affective clues, to discover aspects of what she has done of which she has been unaware. She sees attempts by analysts to maintain emotional distance or presumed objectivity as leading to enactments that are unacknowledged and block genuine growth. She views enactments relating to patient's unconscious fantasies and past experiences as inevitable.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

651

[[RunningHead]]

The Work of Hans Loewald: An Introduction and Commentary

Edited by Gerald I. Fogel. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson Inc. 1991. Pp. 209.

Linda Gibson

Linda Gibson

Linda L. Gibson
1658 N. Meramec, Suite 105, St. Louis, Missouri 63105

Linda Gibson  

The centrepiece of this volume is Loewald's paper 'On the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis', first presented in 1957 but not published until 1960. Fogel regards this article as Loewald's nuclear paper, in which are found, either explicitly or implicitly, the principles which Loewald subsequently elaborated and refined in his later work. At Loewald's request, two of his later papers, 'Superego and time' (1962) and 'Psychoanalysis as an art and the fantasy character of the psychoanalytic situation' (1975), are also included.

After a brief prefatory chapter by the editor, the reader encounters or, as will be the case for many, re-encounters 'On the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis'. Loewald's purpose, simply stated and subsequently both elegantly and poetically elaborated, is 'to attempt to correlate our understanding of the significance of object relations for the formation and development of the psychic apparatus with the dynamics of the therapeutic process' (p. 16). He proceeds to propose that libidinal tension between a more primitively and a more maturely organised psychic apparatus is crucial for psychic development. Loewald utilises early mother/child interactions as the prototype for the therapeutic action in psychoanalysis, with the analyst/mother now functioning as the representative and mediator of a higher level of organisation to the analysand/child.

In developing his thesis, Loewald challenges the view of the psychic apparatus as a closed system. Initially, he focuses on the conception of the analyst as a detached scientific observer, a mirror upon which the analysand's unconscious is reflected, proposing instead that the analyst functions as 'a co-actor on the analytic stage' (p. 18), a participant in a new object-relationship in which both analysand and analyst undergo successive periods of psychic disorganisation (ego regression) and reorganisation (new consolidation). Whereas a mirror, in the traditional

[[RunningHead]]

The Evolution of Character

By Sylvia Brody and Miriam G. Siegel. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1992. Pp. 553.

Barbara Fajardo

Barbara Fajardo

Barbara Fajardo
645 N. Michigan, 400, Chicago, IL 60611

Barbara Fajardo  

This work is the latest summation of a longitudinal research project that began in 1963. A sample of 131 normal, caucasian, American-born mothers and their normal newborn children was originally selected to study the question of whether maternal responsiveness was stable throughout the first year, and if so, whether types of maternal styles were related to qualities of infant development. This project, funded by the National Institute of Mental Health, had grown out of an interest in the significance of the oral phase for qualities of infant development. The thesis was that gratifications and deprivations experienced in infancy will have lasting impact on perceptual, social, cognitive and drive development. The data were derived from interviews with mother, standardised scales of infant development, and a film of mother feeding her infant. For the most part, the scoring of maternal attitudes and behaviour, from interviews and from the feeding film, was done with subjective clinical rating scales. Findings of this first project were reported in Anxiety and Ego Formation by Sylvia Brody and Sidney Axelrod (New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1970).

Subsequent funding was received from the William T. Grant Foundation to continue studying the development of these children (now a sample of 121) from the age of 2 to 7. The findings were published in Mothers, Fathers, and Children, by the same authors (New York: Int. Univ. Press, 1978). One purpose was to determine the stability of the developmental differences among the children as they had been assessed at the end of their first year, and which had been related to maternal adequacy. A second purpose was to ascertain whether there was continuity in maternal behaviour and attitudes from age one to 7 years. The data were derived from yearly parental interviews, films of mother–child interaction, observations of the children in school, and yearly standardised IQ tests and a projective test, the CAT. The findings, as summarised by the authors, demonstrate a continuity of maternal behaviour and attitudes from infancy, and that the children with the less adequate mothers (as determined from Dr Brody's ratings of feeding in the first year of life) have more unfavourable development than children with more adequate mothers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Interaction and the Analysis of Meaning

By Theodore L. Dorpat and Michael L. Miller. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1992. Pp. 326.

Alan Pollack

Alan Pollack

Alan Pollack
1018R Chestnut Street, Newton Upper Falls, MA 02164

Alan Pollack  

Clinical Interaction and the Analysis of Meaning is a valuable contribution to the ferment which currently seems to be roiling psychoanalysis most vigorously: the significance of interpersonal interaction in theory and practice. Applying a Piagetian framework to the contemporary interactional perspective, Dorpat & Miller present a structuralist developmental theory of psychopathology, and a model for the here–and–now dimension of analytic process. I will summarise their theory, appraise what I consider to be its value, and then register some criticisms.

The mind, as viewed by Dorpat & Miller, is 'a processing system that organizes the elements of lived experience into personally meaningful units of information' (p. 106). It does so by means of cognitive operations which determine the categories of meaning assigned to events. These cognitive processes evolve in an ontogenetic hierarchy, the detailed characterisation of which was Piaget's life's work. At each developmental stage, mental contents are constructed by applying the current cognitive operations to elementary sensory data. Among the mental contents so constructed are representations of the self in interaction with the environment. These are organised into distinct units, called 'schemata', which are abstractions representing patterns or regularities in experienced events.

Schemata are the structures comprising this model of the mind. New experiences are assimilated to, and thus given meaning by, existing schemata. Even more: perception itself is guided by schemata. Because they include a set of expectations about unfolding experience, schemata direct attention to certain sensory data, leaving other data unnoted. Of course, the loop is not closed tight; unexpected aspects of experience can be recognised, whereupon existing schemata may be modified to accommodate these novel features. So far, all this is standard Piaget.

In this book, two types of schemata are distinguished. 'Organising schemata' are the primary representations of self and other. Originating at the dawn of life, they group together such data as the tone, rhythm, and timing of the speech, facial expressions and behaviour of the 'self regulating other', as well as the feelings and internal states–of–being of the self. The sense of self and sense of other are co-created and embodied in these schemata. Throughout life, they organise and regulate the sense of self, and register the immediate, affectively meaningful information about the self in interaction with others.

[[RunningHead]]

Saying Goodbye: A Casebook of Termination in Child and Adolescent Analysis and Therapy

Edited by Anita G. Schmukler. Hillsdale, New Jersey: The Analytic Press. 1991. Pp. 377.

Moisy Shopper

Moisy Shopper

Moisy Shopper
4524 Forest Park, St. Louis, Missouri 63108

Moisy Shopper  

In 1967, Geleerd edited The Child Analyst at Work, which presented a detailed picture of the analytic treatment of children of different ages and pathology by well-known child analysts. This was a crucial contribution since many openly doubted whether child analysis was indeed analysis and whether treatment was at all effective. Schmukler's 1991 volume, with an altogether different goal, nevertheless presents another detailed and focused picture of child analysis. It is a credit to all analysts that, in the ensuing years, the issues have changed.

The editor's preface provides a valuable integration of extensive case reports, giving a brief abstract of each with cross-referencing to other chapters where similar issues are discussed, albeit differently. A discussion of termination cannot proceed intelligently unless presenting complaints, family background, and the course of the analysis is known. Nor can the decisions and interactions involved in a termination process be understood unless the reader has some knowledge of the preceding analysis. As a result, the 'casebook' supplies a detailed and concise picture of an analysis of a child. Unfortunately, there are still many analysts who are more than sceptical that an analytic process can take place in children. While it should not be necessary, I believe that the contributors' efforts furnish an additional documentation of the validity and efficacy of child analysis. This is probably an unintended bonus since the emphasis is solely on termination.

Each author discusses the pertinent literature at the beginning of the case report and again in the concluding discussion. Although clinical material and case reports are emphasised, theory is well represented and integrated into the specific topic. Sugarman reports on the three-year analysis of a girl of 3½ years of age with marked separation problems, masochistic provocative behaviour and tantrums. The decision to terminate seemed reasonable. Despite the analyst's wish to be flexible and create a balance between her separation anxiety and her counterphobic defences, a striking reaction occurred once the matter was broached. In deference to her wishes for autonomy, Sugarman decided unilaterally to adopt a gradual reduction of sessions over the next six months. Major themes, interpretations, and reconstructions are reported.

[[RunningHead]]

Conrad Ferdinand Meyer and Freud: The Beginnings of Applied Psychoanalysis

By Alexander Grinstein. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1992. Pp. 399.

Julius Silberger

Julius Silberger

Julius Silberger
172 Buckminster Road, Brookline, Massachusetts 02146

Julius Silberger  

Conrad Ferdinand Meyer (1825-1898) was a Swiss-German poet and novelist, whose publications during the period 1871-89 were quite popular, and who used narratives of history as a way of expressing aspects of his own personality and life experience. His most common themes were conflicts about loyalty to his father, attachment to and fear of his mother, and erotic attachment and distrust of his sister. He became psychotic and required hospitalisation on two occasions in his life; firstly, in 1852, when he was 27, after several years of self-seclusion, and then again, in 1892, when he was 77, at the end of his period of literary productivity.

Meyer's father died when he was 15, and his mother, who had often been depressed, killed herself in 1856, when he was 31. His sister was for many years the manager of the household and also his editor. Meyer married when he was 50. His wife was 38, wealthy, with good connections in Zurich society and they had one child, a daughter. His wife and his sister were in sharp conflict, particularly expressed in their differences about the importance of his writing; his wife seeing it as destructive to his peace of mind, and his sister as vital to the expression of his life's purpose.

Wilhelm Fliess brought Meyer to Sigmund Freud's attention in 1892, when he and his new wife sent Sigmund and Martha Freud a copy of Meyer's Gedichte. Freud made brief references to Meyer in his letters to Fliess during the period 1898-1900—perhaps the most intense one of his self-analysis—and in one of those letters included a somewhat more extended commentary on Meyer's novella The Judge (Die Richterin). He thought of writing more extensively on the relationship between Meyer's life and his stories and read a biography of Meyer written by one of his friends, but was disappointed at its lack of useful detail. Then he no longer seemed interested, but he did include one of Meyer's narrative poems among a list of ten good books solicited from him by a bookseller in 1907, and when a paper on Meyer was discussed at the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society in the same year, Freud indicated that Meyer was still of some interest to him.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Technique in the Treatment of Eating Disorders

Edited by C. Phillip Wilson, Charles C. Hogan and Ira L. Mintz. Northvale, New Jersey and London: Jason Aronson. 1992. Pp. 425.

S. Mogul

S. Mogul

S. Louis Mogul
218 Franklin Street, Newton, Massachusetts 02157

S. Mogul  

This book, the second one edited by this trio, is a collection of 17 chapters by 10 authors unified by their shared belief in a psychodynamic—in many cases psychoanalytic—basis for understanding and treating patients with eating disorders. To this reviewer, the chapters have an unevenness of clarity, readability, and usefulness. C. Phillip Wilson's opening 56-page 'Ego functions and technique' consists of 35 disparate sections, some of which are further subdivided, like a reference book, presenting a partial literature review but mainly Wilson's ideas about aetiology and treatment, illustrated with his clinical material. There are interesting and potentially valuable ideas, but the tone is often polemical, with an emphasis on authoritative, formulaic interpretations of the symbols of primitive drives and corresponding conflicts. A brief passage, quoted out of context, conveys this tone: 'Stone symbolism develops in connection with the process of teething in the 6th to 12th month. Oral-incorporative conflicts, the wish to devour and to be devoured, are repressed and stone comes to symbolize the primitive object relation with the mother. Basic trust, reliability, and object constancy are symbolized by stone … Stone, which is the most universal and plastic of all symbols, symbolizes impulse control, whether it represents teeth, the baby, the mother, the breast, teeth, bladder, feces, vagina, or phallus' (p. 22).

Charles C. Hogan's 'The adolescent crisis in anorexia nervosa' is clearer and more unified. Hogan enriches his psychoanalytic dynamic perspective by recognising the challenging epidemiologcal and social issues in anorexia and bulimia and then integrates this with a developmental perspective based on Helene Deutsch's seminal contribution to female development (The Psychology of Women, Volume 1. New York: Grune & Stratton, 1944). His sensitivity to the particular humiliation adolescents experience in disclosing their inner lives conveys a sense of real clinical experience in contrast to the more drive-centred theoretical views that are abundant in this volume.

[[RunningHead]]

Telling Facts: History and Narration in Psychoanalysis

Edited by Joseph H. Smith & Humphrey Morris. Baltimore and London: Johns Hopkins University Press. 1992. Pp. 307.

Donald Spence

Donald Spence

Donald P. Spence
Robert Wood Johnson Medical School, Piscataway, NJ 08854

Donald Spence  

From the post-modern perspective of the waning years of the twentieth century, a book which is titled Telling Facts may sound a little too innocent for its own good. As we begin to realise that truth is in the telling as much as in what is being told, the standing of a simple fact becomes that much more difficult to establish. If the analysand does indeed tell facts, it is not always clear where they begin and end, how much the fact is largely in the mind of the analyst, and how much it differs from a previous telling with perhaps a different emphasis. Because facts in the analytic setting almost always imply memories, they can never be merely 'shown' during a session, and much recent writing has focused on the different kinds of discourse.

Does the analysand talk as if he/she were alone in the room, or is the story clearly being told to someone else? Perhaps certain facts only appear in the first (monologue) model because the patient feels less embarrassed if the analyst is made to seem not present. Other facts, perhaps less revealing or more self-reinforcing, are clearly told to another. Modes of telling, in turn, affect the memory of both analyst and analysand, and some forms of telling may, in effect, take the event entirely out of the here-and-now and erase it from the memories of those present. As recorded cases are gradually gaining currency, we begin to see ways of comparing what was said with what was remembered (as in process notes) and understand something more of the way in which modes of telling can influence the gist of published case studies.

Many of these issues surface in one or more chapters of this book, sometimes in slightly different language or changes of emphasis. Several authors focus on the question of whether the stories told by the analysand are translations or transformations of the something being described. Two authors (Humphrey Morris and Cynthia Chase) choose the first option, with its connotations of reversibility and veridicality and its echoes of one of Freud's favourite metaphors in The Interpretation of Dreams. To think of the analytic surface as a translation of the various pieces of the patient's world is not only to hold out the hope that we will, in the course of treatment, be able to decipher the code and recover the original text but, in addition, suggests that the underlying changes have a kind of lawfulness that we associate with other parts of the natural world.

responseSet.title: Two Discussions of 'The Mind of the Analyst' and a Response from Madeleine Baranger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Discussions of 'The Mind of the Analyst' and a Response from Madeleine Baranger

Janice De Saussure

Janice De Saussure

Janice De Saussure
Rue de la Tertasse 2
1204 Geneva

Janice De Saussure  

I should like to thank Madeleine Baranger for presenting her concept of the dynamic working relationship established between analyst and analysand. She very perceptively describes it as a special field of interaction which is continuously being created, which involves more for analysands than just a repetition of past relationships and which is constantly influencing both participants in the psychoanalytic process. When I was invited to participate in this discussion, I was given the choice of either discussing Madeleine Baranger's paper directly or giving a position statement. As the topic is so vast, I though it might be more useful for me to give some supplementary ideas, focusing attention on some specific thoughts, feelings and attitudes which are particular to the way we function mentally and emotionally as analysts.

Being a psychoanalyst is an exceedingly challenging and demanding profession. We offer a unique form of therapy which requires us to develop very special skills and attitudes in addition to acquiring a thorough understanding of psychoanalytic concepts, From the moment of our first encounter with a person asking for help we constantly have in mind our basic premise, namely that oedipal and pre-oedipal desires, which have been repressed but not renounced, frequently become sources of symptoms and mental disturbances which dominate and often cripple the lives of adults. This concept involves recognition of the mysterious and almost unbelievable power of unconscious and preconscious mental functioning to prevent individuals from attaining their conscious desires. We do not assume that these are the only causes of symptoms and problems but they are the ones for which we offer help. In contrast to those who practise other forms of psychotherapy, our constant and repetitive task is almost exclusively concentrated on perceiving and communicating the countless ramifications of these repressed desires and of the habitual reactions which have been developed as part of disguised efforts to gratify them. We expect that the compulsive nature of these ways of thinking, feeling and behaving will cause them to be repeated in transference reactions stimulated by their relation to us. Although we seek to understand the personal historical development of these patterns in each analysand, our main concern is with their effects in the present as they become evident in the vicissitudes of the transference.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Anthropology after Freud: Essays Marking the Fiftieth Anniversary of Freud's Death

Edited by David H. Spain. New York: Psyche Press. 1992. Pp. 332.

Maurice Apprey

Maurice Apprey

Maurice Apprey
University of Virginia School of Medicine
2805 Brookmere Road, Charlottesville, VA 22901

Maurice Apprey  

David Spain has assembled an excellent collection of essays in the field of psychoanalytic anthropology to mark the fiftieth anniversary of Freud's death. The features of this book contribute to the definition of the field.

The first five chapters, which constitute Part One, subtitled 'Field and Clinic-Based Studies', more or less reflect the definition of psychoanalytic anthropology as 'that subdiscipline of anthropology devoted to the use of psychoanalytic concepts, method and theory in the study of culture' (p. 2). Most of the remaining six chapters, which constitute Part Two, 'Theoretical and Critical Studies', illustrate another purpose of psychoanalytic anthropology, 'namely, using the ethnographic and theoretical works of anthropology to foster the development and clarification of psychoanalytic theory' (p. 2). These essays allow us to see how psychoanalytic thought can infuse psychoanalytic anthropology and in turn be invigorated by ethnographic findings. These papers are an amalgam of epistemic conversations rather than a collection of homogeneous essays that strive to see one central foundation. Accordingly they show us how these diverse contributions treat theoretical and orienting assumptions as heuristic tools.

How are these heuristic tools presented? Robert Levine's lead essay on 'The self in African culture' is a first-rate exegesis of how our theoretical pretexts discipline and shape our research and inform our findings. His ethnographic research is influenced to some considerable degree by Kohut's psychoanalytic self psychology and Kohut's reading of basic Freudian principles, which suggest that a practitioner 'clarify the surface before going to the depths' (p. 39). In other words, 'a surface psychology, i.e.

responseSet.title: Writer's Block: By Zachary Leader.  Baltimore:  Johns Hopkins University Press.  1991.  Pp. 324.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Writer's Block

By Zachary Leader. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press. 1991. Pp. 324.

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons

Michael Parsons
2 Quadrant Grove, London NW5 4JN

Michael Parsons  

The uneasy relationship between psychoanalysts and students of literature has improved over the years. From 'We must … object to the conclusions of Freud and Dr. Jones on the ground that their proponents do not have an adequate conception of what an artistic meaning is' (Trilling, 1950), we have moved to 'There is now no alternative to reading literature in some relation to psychoanalysis' (Easthope, 1989). Zachary Leader uses psychoanalysis to understand the literature that we cannot read because it does not get written.

Leader is an academic in the field of English literature, with an impressive knowledge of psychoanalysis. As a postgraduate student he found himself comparing Blake with Coleridge and Wordsworth: the one fluently productive, the others plagued with inhibitions to their creativity. This set him wondering about the whole question of blocked creativity in writers, and the result is this book. It reads as a fine conjunction of psychoanalysis and literary scholarship, and reviewing it as a psychoanalyst I wish I had the knowledge to engage more thoroughly with the academic side of it. Leader keeps his own thought processes, his fascination with the topic and his engagement with the task of writing about it, in full view. The experience of reading the book thus has several layers.

Academically and psychoanalytically it is full of interest. But one also sees the author working to assemble it, not without difficulty; and for anyone who himself has tried to write, the book is bound to set up a dialogue with his own experience. I say 'assembling' the book because it has something of a patchwork quality. Leader is interested in so many different aspects of the question of what stops writers writing that the book lacks a certain unity. It is more a collection of essays arranged around a central idea. Leader is aware of this, half apologises for it once or twice, and gets on with what he is doing. The central idea is strong enough, however, and the various perspectives on it interesting enough, for the book to remain thoroughly satisfying.

responseSet.title: Mario Montesano Montessori (1921-1993)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mario Montesano Montessori (1921-1993)

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani
38 Penshurst Gardens
Edgware
Middlesex HA8 9TP

Adam Limentani  

Mario Montesano Montessori died in Amsterdam on 25 February 1993. He was born in Barcelona on 22 April 1921, the second son of four children of an Italian father and an American mother. As the grandson of Maria Montessori, who became internationally known for her work in education, it is not surprising that he should have taken up psychology and, in due course, psychoanalysis.

Montessori once described his upbringing as 'international', owing to his father having been the personal assistant and general manager to his mother. This work entailed extensive travelling, international training courses, congresses, etc. in many countries, and the children went to school wherever they happened to be. At first, the family resided in Barcelona, but had to leave at the outbreak of the Spanish civil war because of their Italian nationality. Their subsequent move to Italy was none too happy, as Maria Montessori's views on liberal education met with strong hostility from the fascist regime. Chance circumstances took the family to the Netherlands, where Montessori resided from 1936, obtaining Dutch nationality in 1950.

An early desire to emigrate to Indonesia prompted him to take a degree in agriculture, but the Second World War brought his dreams of outdoor life to an abrupt end. He then joined the resistance movement, securing work at the same time in a psychological setting.

Having taken his M.A. in psychology in 1949, he started his training in psychoanalysis in 1950, continuing to work as a school psychologist. This did not prevent him from acting as his grandmother's secretary, as well as helping her to complete some scientific work she had undertaken in India.

Soon after graduating as a member of the Dutch Psychoanalytic Society in 1956 he took up his first administrative post as its secretary (1957-62). On achieving training analyst status, he was elected a member of the Education Committee and, in due course, its Vice-Chairman and Chairman (1965-69).

The 24th IPA Congress, held in Amsterdam in 1965, was an opportunity for Montessori to engage in international work. The President, Dr P. J. van der Leuuw, appointed him IPA secretary, a position that involved extensive travelling. At the end of his term of office, four years later, the new President, Dr L. Rangell, then appointed him Associate Secretary for Europe (1969). He was elected Vice-President in 1973, a position he held until 1979.

responseSet.title: Freud's Oratorical Marathon: The Shrinking of Another Myth
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's Oratorical Marathon: The Shrinking of Another Myth

Patrick Mahony Ph.D.

Patrick Mahony

Patrick J. Mahony, Ph.D.
1297 St. Viateur
Outremont, P-Q, H2V IZ2

Patrick Mahony  

Dear Mr Tuckett,

While describing the First Psychoanalytic Congress at Salzburg in 1908, Jones spotlighted Freud's presentation of the Rat Man case. In particular, Jones glorified Freud's oratorical capacity to enthral his audience over a period of some five hours:

'[Freud] sat at the end of a long table along the sides of which we were gathered and spoke in his usual low but distinct conversational tone. He began at the Continental hour of eight in the morning and we listened with rapt attention. At eleven he broke off, suggesting we had enough. But we were so absorbed that we insisted on his continuing, which he did until nearly one o'clock. Someone who can hold an audience engrossed for five hours must have something very worthwhile to say' (Jones, 1955, p. 42).

Jones's temporal account of Freud's oratorical marathon has been frequently repeated, even in my own book on the Rat Man (Mahony, 1986). Extant documentation, however, indicates that Freud's performance was impressive, but not superhuman. It was twenty-five years after the Salzburg Congress that Jones asked Freud: 'What, in your recollection was the duration of your Vortrag on Zwangsneurose at the Salzburg Congress? I find an enormous discrepancy between Sadger's memory and my own' (Freud & Jones, 1993, p. 711). Freud's reply was quite modest: 'As far as I remember, that lecture of mine in Salzburg lasted a terribly long time: 1½ - 2¼ hours? I could no longer do that today' (p. 712).

Freud's temporal estimate is borne out in an early letter that Abraham wrote to him: 'I still remember with great pleasure the two hours taken up by your paper at Salzburg' (Freud & Abraham, 1965, p. 146). The ironical poetic justice of Abraham's letter is that while evidentially demystifying Freud's performance, it testifies to the soundness of his memory a quarter-century after the fact!

responseSet.title: Fifth IPA Conference of Training Analysts
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fifth IPA Conference of Training Analysts

D. Werman M.D.

D. Werman

D. S. Werman, M.D.
Department of Psychiatry Duke University Medical Center
Durham, North Carolina 27710

D. Werman  

Dear Sir:

In Wallerstein's summary of the Fifth IPA Conference of Training Analysts, the word training, not surprisingly, occurs in virtually every paragraph.

It is striking at a time when many psychoanalytic educators are urging that the view of psychoanalytic institutes as trade schools be abandoned in favor of regarding them as centers of graduate-level studies, that the word training is still current. In English, typically, training relates narrowly to technical educational processes—when it is not applied to schools of animal obedience or to the toilet competency of children. In most other languages, the words used, in lieu of training (formation —French, Bildung —German, etc.) connote and denote processes of intellectual development and growth.

Perhaps the word training is too ingrained in English psychoanalytic jargon to be budged, and my view that we should, instead, speak and write of psychoanalytic education, didactic or educational analysts, etc. is utopian. Still, if language has some dialectical relationship to psyche, perhaps what may appear to some as merely an academic caveat is worthy of further discussion.

June 1993

1271

responseSet.title: Contribution to Discussion of Pre-Published Papers at the 38th Congress of the IPA
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contribution to Discussion of Pre-Published Papers at the 38th Congress of the IPA

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner
1040 Park Avenue
New York, NY 10028

Charles Brenner  

The Editor of the Journal has kindly invited me to submit some comments to be included in the report of the Amsterdam meeting. What follows is an expanded version of remarks I made from the floor at the time of the meeting.

During the three mornings devoted to the topic, 'The Mind Of The Analyst: From Listening To Interpretation', we were privileged to hear three pre-published papers and twice that number of prepared discussions, as well as comments from the floor and unprepared responses by the panellists themselves both to each other and to the audience. It was interesting to compare the three pre-published papers. Dr Jacobs kept closely to the assigned topic. He told us, as best he could remember, what had gone on in his mind while listening to a patient prior to making an interpretive intervention. Mrs Baranger spoke more generally. She spoke about the general principles that should guide an analyst while listening to a patient prior to making an interpretation. Her recommendations were based very largely on the concept that analysis consists in understanding and interpreting the nature of an interactional field that exists between patient and analyst and that can be properly described as a shared unconscious phantasy. Dr Duncan, like Dr Jacobs, illustrated the points he wished to make with clinical material from an analytic case. He started from the thesis that all of the current psychoanalytic theories of mental functioning have validity or, in other words, that psychoanalytic theory is at present pluralistic and properly so. The consequence is that, in practice, one uses them all, each in its proper place. Dr Duncan finds Kleinian theory useful in understanding some things, object-relations theory useful for others, self psychology for still others, and so on.

In arguing thus, it is evident that Dr Duncan introduced a new topic into the discussion, a topic much discussed in recent years. Is psychoanalytic theory properly pluralistic? Should we accept all of the current theories with equal credence and, if we do, should we be guided, now by one and now by another, in our clinical work?

responseSet.title: Melanie Klein: From Theory to Reality: By Otto Weininger.  London:  Karnac Books.  1992.  Pp. 210.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein: From Theory to Reality

By Otto Weininger. London: Karnac Books. 1992. Pp. 210.

R. Hinshelwood

R. Hinshelwood

R. D. Hinshelwood
18 Artesian Road, London W2 5AR

R. Hinshelwood  

Professor Weininger is an enthusiast; this is the latest in a series of his expositions of Melanie Klein's work. He has identified, rightly, that Klein's writing often lacks a reader-friendly style—while for Kleinians she is inspiring, for others she seems so frequently offputting. Klein herself remarked a little despairingly, in describing her chosen area of interest, that there is a 'great handicap, for these phantasies arise at a time when the infant has not yet begun to think in words'.

Weininger has decided to grasp the nettle. All his books are attempts to move, as the subtitle of this one suggests, from the bones of theory to the flesh and blood of experience. In this book, he describes Melanie Klein's theories of just those remote unconscious phantasies, and he attempts to substitute words for whatever the preverbal infant uses for 'thinking' and 'communicating'. This is a courageous venture and therefore it risks the very same response that Melanie Klein's own writing often attracts: 'It's all very well but how does she know; and anyway I can't believe that babies have those sorts of feelings and phantasies'.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

860

responseSet.title: Editorial Appointments for 1994
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial Appointments for 1994

David Tuckett and Tom Hayley

Tom Hayley

Tom Hayley  

The present editorial team's tenure of office will finish with the final part of this volume at the end of this year. It is not always understood that all the editorial appointments to the International Journal, with no exception, are honorary and we wish to express here our deep appreciation for the work undertaken by the Editors for North America, Europe and Latin America together with the members of the outstanding Boards they have been able to assemble. Dr Vann Spruiell, Mrs Anne-Marie Sandler and Dr Horacio Etchegoyen and Dr Jorge Ahumada have not only given unstintingly of their own time and effort but have also been able to inspire colleagues on their respective Boards to carry out the task. We shall have more to say about them and their achievement at the appropriate time when we conclude all our terms later in the year.

Meanwhile, this is to announce that the British Psycho-Analytical Society has decided that in future, when there is a change of editors in London, as there will be now, the customary procedure whereby the new editor or editors have appointed (or re-appointed) colleagues in North America, Europe and Latin America on the basis of an informal system of advice, should be changed. The increasing size and complexity of our psychoanalytic institutions, developments in regional organisation and representation, and the appointment of Editorial Boards to advise our regional editors (begun first by Dr Edward Weinshel in North America and then accelerated by the present incumbents), have all combined to convince us that despite the very considerable merits of the old informal systems and their excellent results, the time has come to formalise the way in which editors are chosen. In future, when there is a new Editor in London, the appointment of Editors for North America, Latin America and Europe, will be made after a regional search committee has been appointed to canvass legitimate opinion among relevant societies and institutes. Existing editors who have served less than ten years will be eligible for re-appointment.

responseSet.title: All the Mothers are One: Hindu India and the Reshaping of Psychoanalysis: By Stanley N. Kurtz, New York:  Columbia University Press.  1992.  Pp. 306.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

All the Mothers are One: Hindu India and the Reshaping of Psychoanalysis

By Stanley N. Kurtz, New York: Columbia University Press. 1992. Pp. 306.

Theodore Lidz

Theodore Lidz

Theodore Lidz
Dept. of Psychiatry
25 Park Street, New Haven, CT 06519

Theodore Lidz  

The book could as well be entitled 'All the Goddesses are One', for the author relates the Hindu religious beliefs and myths to family transactions, and in the process suggests how psychoanalysis can be modified to suit cultures other than the Western civilisation in which it was born. Unfortunately, Kurtz seems to believe that he is the first to have suggested that modifications apply to a different culture, and he ignores Takeo Doi's and Keigo Okonogi's modifications for Japanese analysis, as well as those concerning the cultures of Papua New Guinea, and of the influence of collective child-rearing on the Kibbutzim of Israel, all of which have pertinence to his thesis.

The author went to India to study the characteristics of a new goddess, Santoshi Ma, who first emerged in the latter half of this century and has become extremely popular. Kurtz rightly expected that the creation of a new goddess, a rarity in India, indicated some shift in the social structure which he would be able to relate to some special characteristics of Santoshi Ma. To his dismay, his potential informants refused to talk about Santoshi Ma as an individual goddess, and when directed to her shrine, Kurtz found that it was the shrine of an old goddess, Vaishno Devi. He was repeatedly informed that Santoshi Ma was not an individual goddess, but that 'all goddesses are one'—apparently manifestations of the Great Goddess, or the comprehensive religious spirit. As far as he could determine, Santoshi Ma's rapid rise had resulted from a very popular film about her. Kurtz did not give up, but sought to understand what was meant by 'all the goddesses are one', which suggested, in the light of his psychoanalytic knowledge, that it had to do with the nature of Hindu mothering. His pursuit of further understanding gave rise to this fascinating, but rather perplexing book.

Stated very briefly, Kurtz attributes the Hindu concept that all mothers and all goddesses are one to the rearing of children in conjoint families, in which the care of small children is shared with in-laws and the child must learn early in life that he or she has several mothers and does not learn to individuate, but rather to become part of a group, the family group. Further, Kurtz refutes the conclusions of analytically-oriented psychiatrists, primarily Morris Carstairs (who, incidentally, was not an analyst) and Sudhir Kakar, that various Hindu traits are related to Hindu mothers' over-solicitude with minimal frustration of the small child. Rather, he found that although the mothers were extremely attentive, it was without emotion and displays of affection. Moreover, mothers were not attentive to the child when a parent-in-law was present. At an early age, the child had the unconscious task of fusing mother-figures and becoming part of the family group.

responseSet.title: Truth and Reality in Psychoanalytic Discourse
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Truth and Reality in Psychoanalytic Discourse

Victoria Hamilton Ph.D.

Victoria Hamilton

Victoria Hamilton, Ph.D.
990 Hanley Avenue
Los Angeles
California 90049

Victoria Hamilton  

Dear Sirs,

Thank you for your invitation to respond to Evelyne Schwaber's letter of 29 March in which Dr Schwaber seeks to correct a 'significant misrepresentation' of her work as portrayed in my paper 'Truth and reality in psychoanalytic discourse' (IJPA, 1993, 1). First, I wish to clarify that the phrase 'clear and equal glass' which occurs in quotation marks in my paper is not from Dr Schwaber's work. The phrase is used by some philosophers of mind to describe a view of the mind as 'a glassy essence' which mirrors reality. (The quote, cited below, is from a passage in Francis Bacon's Advancement of Learning.1 A full discussion of 'our glassy essence' occurs in the book Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature by Richard Rorty.)

In my paper, I referred to Dr Schwaber's work in order to illustrate a particular methodological approach to clinical practice. I described this 'listening stance' as 'radically empiricist'. The quotations in Dr Schwaber's letter are aimed to correct this description. My response aims to address both the complex issues raised by Dr Schwaber and the viewpoint discussed in my paper in which the reference to Dr Schwaber's work occurred.

I think it would be fair to deduce from the excerpts quoted in her letter that Dr Schwaber supports a constructivist theory of reality and a relativist theory of truth. She does not believe in 'theory-free data', 'objective reality' or truth by correspondence. Expanding on Freud's distinction between 'material' and 'psychic' reality, Schwaber states: 'what we can know of the world is by our own ways of seeing it'; 'our participation, as may be expressed in the ways in which we use our varying models, is intrinsic to the reality we are to discern and to articulate'. And with respect to truth, Schwaber enjoins us to 'locate the patient's vantage-point', his 'truth' outside the analyst's position of truth-'teaching' or reality 'arbitration' for 'real is how things are felt to be and how they are perceived'. 'Real is what each of us experiences as true—the correctness of which no other one of us can be the arbiter' (IJPA, 1990, p. 238).

responseSet.title: Transference, Counter Transference and Acting out in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference, Counter Transference and Acting out in Psychoanalysis

Abraham De Blécourt

Abraham De Blécourt

Abraham De Blécourt
153 Gerrit van der Veenstrat
1077 DZ Amsterdam

Abraham De Blécourt  

SUMMARY

The development of the transference concept is described and studied from structural, genetic, dynamic and adaptive points of view. A sketch is given of the development of a transference neurosis during treatment, and of the difference between transference phenomena and transference neurosis. A number of examples from practice are given, also in connection with the occurring repetition compulsion. In the discussion of the countertransference concept a distinction is made between the so-called classic approach and the totalistic approach to this concept.

The author studies the possible interference of the countertransference during treatment. If the analyst can withdraw from his trial identification with his patient no interference will occur. Sudden countertransference phenomena may be eliminated by self-analysis. Chronic countertransference phenomena can only be solved by re-analysis.

Examples from practice are given and the fact is demonstrated that analyst and patient are not only transference–countertransference objects for each other, but also real objects.

Attention is paid to the old and the new concept of acting out. Examples from practice are given of acting out during and outside the analytic sessions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Rani of Chitor: The Face

Fabio Herrmann

Fabio Herrmann

Fabio Herrmann
Rua Agrário de Souza, 106
01445 São Paulo

Fabio Herrmann  

SUMMARY

In this paper the author introduces a psychoanalytic concept: the face. This concept has two aspects: it is the particular form in which the subject's identity is revealed to him, and it also demonstrates how the analyst works in the sense of allowing the patient to discover who he is. For this purpose the author makes use of a Hindu legend, which he does not interpret conventionally but narrates, inviting the reader to learn with it. The foundations of this approach lie in the results of his research on the psychoanalytic method and in his consequent position with respect to psychoanalysis: field theory. Fixed interpretive keys are not used; the material, the legend, throws light on an aspect of psychoanalysis itself, while psychoanalysis illuminates the material. The theoretical and methodological reorganisation suggested here helps us to understand the nature of psychoanalytic interpretation and how analysis works in the investigation of the face of human desire.
