responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Towards Reading Freud: Self-Creation in Milton, Wordsworth, Emerson, and Sigmund Freud.

By Mark Edmundson. Princeton: Princeton University Press. 1990. Pp. 171.

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber

Paul Schwaber
258 Bradley Street, New Haven, Connecticutt 06511

Paul Schwaber  

Every psychoanalyst who has felt the weight of too much precedent—of Freud's heavy company in a new clinical venture, for example, or the burden of citing him in every article for publication—will understand the urgency of this intriguing book by Mark Edmundson. Psychoanalysts also know the pleasures of reading Freud—the memorable clinical instances and theoretical daring, the freshness of address because his distinct presence is inscribed in the writing, as he thinks dialogically and invites others to join in. Edmundson, who teaches English at the University of Virginia, responds acutely to both these quite opposite effects Freud generates; he worries too that in the culture at large and in actual clinical practice the constraining influence Freud exerts has overwhelmed the example of self-realisation he affords. So it is as an heroic and helpful but nonetheless baleful influence—as a hindrance to human possibilities—that Freud emerges here.

Edmundson wishes to consider Freud's writings anew and to study them critically, freed of the resistance posed by accumulated interpretations and assumptions. He proposes, therefore, to approach them from a perspective of incongruity, by way of imaginative literature, and proceeds to conjoin transformational works of Freud's with others from great writers—some of whom Freud had read and some he hadn't but all of whom articulated vivid representations of self-creation. Like Freud he then ponders Oedipus Rex and Hamlet at the centre of the founding document of psychoanalysis, The Interpretation of Dreams. Subsequently he juxtaposes 'On narcissism' with passages from Paradise Lost, the technique papers with Wordsworth, and 'Mourning and melancholia' with Emerson. And by such indirection he finds direction out: Edmundson ventilates crucial moments in the development of Freud's thought.

He discerns a characterising tension in Freud's works between 'romantic' and 'normative' tendencies, between the singular Freud one gets to know reading them and all the oedipally- and narcissistically-configured others he describes and defines. Freud the romantic aims at originality, at self-creation. He is transgressive, the exception. Normative Freud, in contrast, accepts and even imposes human limits. He 'demonstrates repeatedly how small the area is in which reason can hope to hold command'. This Freud aims 'to acquaint his readers and his patients with the causes for their sufferings … to show them the way to psychological health' (p. ix). The two Freuds—the one heroic and individualistic, the other therapeutic and regulating—emerge dialectically in the dream book, where Freud presents himself as the central dreamer and imposing scientist who solves the age-old enigma of dreams and patiently and lucidly expounds his discoveries. His interpretations lead, of course, to a general psychology—a norm. Edmundson argues that the special accomplishment Freud manages in the dream book is that in achieving the unprecedented himself, he concomitantly and necessarily delimits his readers and patients into 'Freudian' subjects, because he establishes the organising theory (or mythology) of psychological development that is now part of our most ordinary assumptions. By explaining the manner and meaning of Oedipus Rex and Hamlet, furthermore, Freud dramatises his comparability to Sophocles and Shakespeare as well as the triumph of psychoanalysis over literature as the standard of truth. Thus the logic of Freud's exposition and self-presentation in the dream book leads to his own uniqueness—to the only self not figured as Freudian—and to his authority.

[[RunningHead]]

Cuarenta Años De Psicoanálisis En Chile. Biografía De Una Sociedad Científica

(Forty Years of Psychoanalysis in Chile. The Biography of a Scientific Society.) Edited by E. Casaula, J. Colona and J. F. Jordan. Santiago de Chile: Editorial Ananké. 1991. Pp. 957 (2 volumes).

Elias Rocha Barros []

Elias Rocha Barros

Elias Mallet Da Rocha Barros
Rua Bahia 71 apto. 7A, São Paulo, CEP 01244, Brazil

Elias Rocha Barros  

It is no easy task to review a two-volume book consisting of 45 articles written in a language not available to most of the Journal's readers. The first temptation to be avoided is that of presenting a summary of some of the more significant papers, as the result would very likely be long, confusing and boring. The nearly 1000 pages of this work are witness to the vitality of the Chilean psychoanalytic movement, a living history of its influences, and an opportunity to see how psychoanalysis forms part of the Latin-American cultural context.

In the prologue, the editors cite Proust's classic passage on the memories that were brought back by the flavour of the madeleines dipped in tilleiul (lime). All of Cambrai, its surroundings, its gardens and the atmosphere of Proust's house rise up from the cup of tea. This is the beautiful introduction of what is to come. As I see it, this is a vivid metaphor for the history of the influence of European psychoanalysis on Latin-American culture. I believe that a psychoanalysis based on the understanding of emotional experiences and the search for their meaning, resulting from relations between psychic objects that take place in an interior world, would arouse a feeling of familiarity in any Latin-American. Our myths, legends and culture favour intense emotional experiences and thus are fertile ground for theories that give a central place to the emotions and an internal world where meanings are produced.

One of the reasons why Kleinian thinking has become so widespread in Latin America seems to be the importance it gives to emotional experiences. These are seen as giving meaning to mental life; they operate as a kind of connective tissue that structures the individual's internal world. This idea that there is an interior world where emotional experiences can be thought out finds an immediate echo in Latin-Americans' emotional and intellectual life. It is there that emotions take on meaning and are perceived as being at least as real as the external world. In our literature, the works of García Márques, Julio Cortázar, Machado de Assis, Guimarães Rosa and Jorge Amado, to name but a few, are vivid witnesses to this culture of the internal world and the importance that the search for meaning has in our emotional experiences.

[[RunningHead]]

A Critique of Psychoanalytic Reason: Hypnosis as a Scientific Problem from Lavoisier to Lacan

By Leon Chertok and Isabelle Stengers, translated from the French by Martha Noel Evans. Stanford, CA: Stanford University Press. 1992. Pp. 319.

Louise Carignan

Louise Carignan

Louise Carignan
275 MacLaren, Ottawa, Ontario, Canada K2P 0L9

Louise Carignan  

The foundations of psychoanalysis are questioned by many voices today, speaking both from inside and outside the field. A Critique Of Psychoanalytic Reason combines the perspectives of an analyst devoted to research in hypnosis and a philosopher of science. The authors re-examine Freud's foundation of the psychoanalytic method within the evolving epistemological context of modern science. They argue that Freud's endeavours to construct a science of the irrational have failed and that this failure forces us to reconsider the scientific model which guided him.

In summary: (1) Freud abandoned hypnosis to create a more reliable and objective technique.

responseSet.title: Anny Katan (1898-1992)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anny Katan (1898-1992)

Robert Furman

Robert Furman

Robert A. Furman
2869 Colchester Road
Cleveland
Ohio 44106

Robert Furman  

Dr Anny Katan (née Rosenberg) died at her home on 24th December 1992. She was 94 years old. Her family were friends of the Freuds and she completed her medical and psychoanalytic training in Vienna. After her marriage to Maurits Katan, the Dutch psychoanalyst, they moved to Holland, where both survived the war, he in hiding, she on false papers. Prior to and after leaving Vienna she had been most active in the underground and helped a number of analysts to escape, among them Margaret Mahler, who Dr Anny always said, remained grateful for the rest of her life.

After the war, at the instigation and urging of Anna Freud, the Katans came to Cleveland to assist in the restructuring of the Western Reserve University School of Medicine and to establish psychoanalytic training in Cleveland. Shortly after her arrival she began importing graduates of Miss Freud's Child Therapy Course in Hampstead, who formed around her the centre of the child analytic movement in Cleveland. First came 'The Therapeutic Nursery School', now know as the Hanna Perkins School, in 1950, then the Cleveland Child Therapy Course for training non-medical child analysts in 1958, and in 1964 the Child Analytic Clinic where to this day children may receive a full analysis based strictly on need because of the Clinic's sliding fee scale. In 1966 she was a Founding Trustee of the Cleveland Center for Research in Child Development, the umbrella organisation that oversees operation of all these various programmes in addition to community outreach services and research projects.

In addition to her work in establishing the child analytic research complex in Cleveland, Dr Katan was perhaps best known for her papers on verbalisation, object removal, stranger distress (as opposed to stranger anxiety), distortions of the phallic phase, and adults who had been raped as children.

An era comes close to the end with her death, as we do believe she was the last of that second generation of analysts who gathered about Anna Freud in Vienna and helped establish the foundations of child psychoanalysis. Dr Anny's was a life uncompromisingly dedicated and devoted to psychoanalysis. She always had two wishes, the first being to 'die in harness' and she did continue analytic work into her nineties. Her second was simply to be remembered as 'Anny Katan, psychoanalyst'.

[[RunningHead]]

Religious Objects as Psychological Structures: A Critical Integration of Object Relations Theory, Psychotherapy, and Judaism

By Moshe Halevi Spero. Chicago and London: The Chicago University Press. 1992. Pp. 242.

David Black

David Black

David M. Black
30 Cholmley Gardens, London NW6 1AG

David Black  

One difficulty confronting the psychoanalytic writer who in some way wants to 'defend' religion against the conventional analytic dismissal, is to know what exactly he wants to defend. Is he merely stating the obvious fact that some believers have excellent human qualities and seem to be reasonably non-neurotic? Is he wanting to go further, and say that religious belief has contributed to this happy state of affairs? Does he want to argue (like Jung) that to confront religious issues is an essential element in human self-development or 'wholeness'? Or (like William James) that religious ideas have a 'psychological truth', irrespective of any other truth they may possess? Or does he want to take the religious bull by the horns and say that religion speaks of an objective reality different from, and far greater than, those spoken of by traditional psychoanalysis (which would be, I presume, the position of the 'naive believer')?

In this very scholarly, responsible and passionate book, Moshe Halevi Spero affirms the last of these positions. He writes as an orthodox Jew and a psychoanalytic therapist, and is also clearly a considerable scholar in both of his huge traditions. I am aware that in my comments I shall do no justice to the impressive range of his scholarship.

He begins by dismissing the hermeneutic approach to either religion or psychoanalysis: such an approach, he says (rightly, in my view), costs both science and religion their moral force. But he recognises two sorts of object-representations of God: one derived primarily from human interaction ('god-representations'), the other based on interaction with an objective God ('God-representations').

He is quite uncompromising about God's objective reality and otherness from the believer. God is an 'objective object' with whom we can have a relationship on precise analogy with another person. There is 'an actual form of interchange between a human and a veridically existing divinity' (p. 15),

responseSet.title: Some Dream Mechanisms in Finnegans Wake
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Dream Mechanisms in Finnegans Wake

Olga Cox

Olga Cox

Olga Cox
19 Belgrave Square
Monkstown
County Dublin

Olga Cox  

SUMMARY

This paper does not purport to offer a psychoanalytic reading of Finnegans Wake, but rather to demonstrate how, in recreating the mechanisms of the dream, Joyce's masterpiece offers to psychoanalysis a uniquely rich opportunity to explore the shadowy play of the dream in the permanence of a work of art.

In particular, the manner in which Finnegans Wake tells its story through the distortions of dream narrative while bodying forth its protagonists in the substitutions of dream-identity are explored, with the help of certain Freudian and Lacanian concepts of unconscious structure.

responseSet.title: Primitive Object Love in Melanie Klein's Thinking; Early Theoretical Influences
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Primitive Object Love in Melanie Klein's Thinking; Early Theoretical Influences

Meira Likierman

Meira Likierman

Meira Likierman
5 Downshire Hill
London NW3 1NR

Meira Likierman  

SUMMARY

Melanie Klein's thinking on early object love is pivotal to her vision of development. Yet within her texts it is often enmeshed in complex formulations that obscure its full significance. A greater clarity in viewing Klein's concept of early object love is achieved through tracing some of its historical origins. The author notes that it was inspired by aspects of Ferenczi's theory, hence its similarities with the concept of 'primary love' formulated by another of Ferenczi's analysands, Michael Balint. Ferenczi's thinking on the subject formed part of a broader network of theoretical developments in the history of psychoanalytic ideas. These are shown to have origins in Freud's fluctuations between a 'passive' and a 'genital' characterisation of infantile sexuality. A dialectical tension between these two possibilities, as maintained in the Freudian texts, was partly embodied in the differing outlooks of Klein's two psychoanalysts, Abraham and Ferenczi. The author suggests that it is a mixture of their approaches that partly shaped Klein's thinking on the earliest experiences of love. After outlining these developments the author analyses the nature of early object love as

251

it emerges from Klein's powerful, if intermittent descriptions in the texts. The analysis also tackles a textual ambiguity in Klein's formulations, showing that she explored two kinds of primitive ideal experience without explicitly differentiating them. Such differentiation is important inasmuch as it highlights negative and positive aspects of Kleinian theory at root.

responseSet.title: Splitting of the Ego, as the Central Phenomenon in Neurosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Splitting of the Ego, as the Central Phenomenon in Neurosis

Ritske Le Coultre

Ritske Le Coultre

Ritske Le Coultre  

SUMMARY

The phenomenon of a split in the ego is not confined to fetishism, but is central to all neuroses. The author demonstrates this with regard to several specific neurotic types. He then goes on to show that the attempt to establish an absolute distinction between ego and id postulates an artificial split between—in fact—mutually interdependent parts. Traditional distinctions between primary and secondary process are critically examined. The id is an abstraction, while the essence and strength of the ego reside not in its defensive functions, but in the extent to which it accepts the drives; whereas the repressing ego weakens or even eliminates itself.

The child who cannot resolve psychosexual conflict uses a makeshift solution, whereby that portion of the ego engaged in the conflict is split off and ceases to participate in further development. This part, emotionally rich, may again blossom and mature in the transference.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis in its Cultural Context: Edited by Edward Timms & Ritchie Robertson.  Austrian Studies III.  Edinburgh:  Edinburgh University Press. 1992.  Pp. 209.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis in its Cultural Context

Edited by Edward Timms & Ritchie Robertson. Austrian Studies III. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press. 1992. Pp. 209.

Margot Waddell

Margot Waddell

Margot Waddell
Tavistock Clinic, 120 Belsize Lane, London NW3

Margot Waddell  

This is a specialist book for specialists—a collection of articles, review articles and shorter reviews on current research into the cultural traditions of the Hapsburg Empire and Austrian Republic. The editors' aim is to make this research available to an international readership.

Each volume of Austrian Studies has a particular focus. The first (1990) examines Vienna in 1900 in relation to philosophy— From Altenberg to Wittgenstein; the second (1991), The Austrian Enlightement and its Aftermath. This third volume constitutes a collection of articles which, between them, address two interrelated, relatively neglected and extremely important areas: the contextualisation of psychoanalysis and its historiography.

My concern is with the response of the non-specialist: apart from the content of the individual pieces, what is the place of the larger enterprise in the 'world' of psychoanalysis? The editors' intention is to examine the spirit and the cultural context in which psychoanalysis was conceived. 'It is only by putting Freud back into context that it will be possible to appreciate precisely what he achieved' (p. ix). Assessing whether or not this claim is substantiated is the task of the reviewer.

The approach is a welcome one—part of the more general move towards academic effort being devoted to psychoanalysis on a new scale both inside and outside the community itself. A few representative examples of such a move would include the work of Peter Gay, Peter Fonagy, Riccardo Steiner, André Haynal (Psychoanalysis and the Sciences); Lisa Appignanesi & John Forrester (Freud's Women); Michael Rustin (The Good Society and the Inner World); Freud in Exile (ed. Edward Timms & Naomi Segal); The Freud-Klein Controversies, 1941-45 (ed. Pearl King & Riccardo Steiner).

The opening article argues for the importance of submitting psychanalysis to critical question of the nature of the determinants of how history gets written—which 'history' becomes dominant, and why. Subsequent articles and reviews throw light on this central question—the thesis being that the early history of psychoanalysis, dominated by Freud as both author and subject of that history, has tended to lose sight of the ideas and contributions of 'his dissenting followers and forgotten adversaries'. In so doing the volume raises some interesting historiographical issues which bear on the subsequent institutionalisation, theory and practice of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: The Psychotherapy of the Elderly Self: By Hyman A. Muslin.  New York:  Brunner-Mazel.  1992.  Pp. 220+xviii.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychotherapy of the Elderly Self

By Hyman A. Muslin. New York: Brunner-Mazel. 1992. Pp. 220+xviii.

Ralph Kahana

Ralph Kahana

Ralph J. Kahana
9 Elba Street, Brookline, MA 02146

Ralph Kahana  

The last stages of life are replete with narcissistic trauma. Changes in appearance, recession of sexual function, illness, retirement, increasing need for family and social help, deaths of close relatives and friends, and awareness of limited time, strain psychological resources and lower self-esteem. Dr Muslin brings the perspective of self psychology, centring on narcissism, to the treatment of older persons.

The nine chapters cover a definition of the self; its development and psychopathology in the elderly; diagnosis and planning of therapy; three forms of treatment— supportive, goal-limited and psychoanalysis; the inner world of the therapist; and an epilogue, discussing the fear of ageing. The self is defined broadly as a composite of the different structures of the mind, reflecting a particular culture as well as the individual's physical state. The emphasis in development is placed upon narcissism. Optimally, internalised early parental (selfobject) affirmation endows a child with self-worth; lifelong infusions of approval maintain this positive feeling and, with it, a cohesively functioning self. For the elderly, structural transformation is necessary to achieve a self in harmony with one's actual capacities. The expected psychological changes of ageing, such as slowing, diminished energy and uncertainty of recent memory, often lead to narcissistic vulnerability. Abandoning old ideals in the process of transforming them can be a major loss.

Psychopathology in the elderly is seen as resistance to transforming into a cohesive elderly self—appearing as neuroses particular to ageing, or neuroses and character disorders continued from earlier years. The viewpoint of self psychology is stated unequivocally: 'all forms of psychopathology are derived from defects in … the self or from distortions of the self—attributable to disturbances of self/selfobject relations in childhood … in contrast to classical analysis, the conflicts in the object-instinctual realm … in particular the Oedipus complex … are not the cause of psychopathology but its results …' The central pathological experience is thus a failure of archaic self/selfobject relations, leading to massive loss of self-esteem, followed by anxiety and breakdown of the self. Narcissistic deficits may follow in the form of low energy, joylessness, emptiness, oversensitivity, rage, haughtiness, withdrawal, loneliness, or weakening of assertiveness and ideals. Perhaps the most common disturbances in the aged are episodic fragmentation disorders in reaction to losses and disappointments, which require enhancement of self-esteem through a temporary self/selfobject merger with an empathic caretaker.

responseSet.title: 75th Anniversary of the Journal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

75th Anniversary of the Journal

1994 will see the publication of the 75th volume of the Journal and we intend to celebrate this event both by devoting the final issue of the year to the publication of papers by distinguished international colleagues on the theme of 'The Conceptualisation and Communication of Clinical Facts in Psychoanalysis' and by the holding of three celebratory meetings to which those colleagues who are able to communicate in English will contribute.

We envisage that the theme will be addressed in detail by invited speakers who will contribute to a dialogue on one of the following topics. Each speaker will present his viewpoint in the context of a detailed clinical example. The topics, which address the issues raised by the overall theme, are:

1. What is a Clinical Fact?

2. The Conceptualisation of Clinical Facts.

3. Formulations to the Patient.

4. Validation in the Clinical Process.

5. The Publication of Clinical Facts.

All papers will be in English, which will be the language of each conference. Details of the programme and speakers for each conference will appear in this Journal later in the year. The conferences will be held as follows:

Easter 1994 in New York

To be hosted by the Columbia University Psychoanalytic Center for Training and Research in association with the other New York Psychoanalytic Institute-Societies who belong to the International Psychoanalytical Association, and arranged by an organising committee comprising Helen Meyers (Columbia Center) as Chairperson and Abby Adams-Silvan (New York Freudian Society); Lawrence Chalfin (The Psychoanalytic Institute, New York University Medical Center); Steve Ellman (Institute for Psychoanalytic Training and Research) and Ed Nersessian (New York Psychoanalytic Institute).

October 1994 in London

To be hosted by the British Psycho-Analytical Society as its Conference for English-Speaking Psychoanalysts in 1994 and being arranged by an organising committee chaired by Jennifer Johns of the British Society.

responseSet.title: The Harvard Lectures: By Anna Freud, Edited and annotated by Joseph Sandler.  London:  Institute of Psycho-Analysis and Karnac Books.  1992.  Pp. 142.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Harvard Lectures

By Anna Freud, Edited and annotated by Joseph Sandler. London: Institute of Psycho-Analysis and Karnac Books. 1992. Pp. 142.

Ronald Baker

Ronald Baker

Ronald Baker
12 Southwood Lawn Rood, Highgate, London, Nb 5SF

Ronald Baker  

In 1952, Anna Freud travelled to Harvard University, where she gave a series of lectures to the Department of Social Relations of Radcliffe College. Her audience consisted mainly of a group of about 200 undergraduates and her course was on the psychoanalytic theory of child development, including her views on the upbringing of children. She did not read these lectures and it is thought that she did not refer to notes. Indeed, all the indications are that they were probably not intended for publication.

So, how did this slim volume come about? Clearly, it is not just another opportunity, or excuse, to release some unpublished work, albeit that of a most distinguished psychoanalyst. Far from it. This is Anna Freud as she was recorded on tape, with all the spontaneity and finesse of a confident and unselfconscious expert. Fortunately, these recordings have been lovingly preserved and transcribed and they have only been 'lightly' edited and annotated.

The reader cannot but feel that he is part of an audience with whom he is taken on a singular journey. The emphasis is on a known landscape, there is nothing new in what is explored. For the psychoanalyst cognisant with the rudiments of theory, the regions traversed are familiar. But, as Anna Freud gently warns her audience at the outset, '[this] does not mean always that the facts are easy and not complex'. The terrain of classical psychoanalysis, and especially child development, is comprehensively mapped out in language which, in essence, is simple and direct. Our guide takes us to all the important places and shows us the significant landmarks with the sureness of step of a pioneer. It is difficult not to feel that this time we have chosen a most unusual and gifted escort.

Those who have heard Anna Freud speak will immediately be reminded of her distinctive style of presentation. Those who have not will enjoy an unexpected but welcome opportunity to experience her at her most charismatic.

The reviewer is always placed in a position of particular responsibility and difficulty in transmitting his views of an eminent colleague's work. One does not want to be regarded as being unduly idealising nor unduly critical, yet one must meet the task with an open-minded and honest perspective. Nor does it meet the requirement of a review if one's praise or fault-finding is gratuitous. To be sure, it would be relatively easy to discover and highlight faults and discrepancies in this particular work. But would such an approach be truly relevant to the review? For instance, her grammatical style is highly personal, her reflections occur within the context of psychoanalysis in 1952 and the then current climate of ego psychology and the drives—object-relations theory is given little space, detail is sacrificed in order to maintain comprehension, and so on.

responseSet.title: New Editorial Team
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

New Editorial Team

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

David Tuckett  

A new editorial team starts work with this part of Volume 74. I have become sole Editor, with Dr Jorge L. Ahumada taking over as Editor for Latin America, Dr Arnold M. Cooper as Editor for North America and Dr Jean-Michel Quinodoz as Editor for Europe. Our term of office will last until late in 1998.

It is with very great sadness that I must report that my colleague and previous Joint Editor, Tom Hayley, died on Wednesday 23rd June. He was almost eighty years old and had served first as Editor (1978-1988) and then as Joint Editor, being due to retire this September. He was a marvellous colleague and worked untiringly to improve psychoanalytic scholarship and communication worldwide and he will be greatly missed. An obituary will appear in a later issue.

Dr Horacio Etchegoyen (Editor for Latin America 1989-1993), Mrs Anne-Marie Sandler (Editor for Europe 1987-1993) and Dr Vann Spruiell (Editor for North America 1988-1993) now retire. They have all given unstintingly of their time and energy and have brought their very considerable intellects and abilities to the task. The departure of such highly valued colleagues will inevitably place a burden on the rest of us. Were Tom Hayley still alive I know he would want to join me in thanking all of them very much indeed. They will remain associated with the Journal as Consultant Associate Editors.

Dr Jorge L. Ahumada, our new Editor for Latin America, graduated with honours from the Buenos Aires University School of Medicine in 1962 and subsequently trained as a psychiatrist in both the Jose T. Borda National Hospital in Buenos Aires and the Sheppard-Pratt Hospital in Towson, Maryland. He became an Associate Member of the Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association in 1979, a Full Member in 1982 and a Training Analyst in 1986. He has served in a number of positions in the Buenos Aires Society and has published papers in Psychiatry, the British Journal of Medical Psychology, the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, the International Review of Psycho-Analysis, the Revue Française de Psychanalyse, the Revista Brasileira de Psicanálise and Psicoanálisis (Bs. As). He has previously served with Dr Etchegoyen as Assistant Editor for Latin America and was selected from a short list drawn up by the Search Committee for Latin America appointed by Dr Saul Peña, President of FEPAL. The Search Committee comprised Dr Etchegoyen (Chairman), Dr Eduardo Agejas, Dr Jaime Heresi, and Dr Alfredo Kargieman. Dr Ahumada will now be responsible for establishing a new Editorial Board for Latin America, which rather than being based on the previous representative system, will now be organised like the other regional boards and, therefore, be made up of a variety of distinguished colleagues with a record of published papers. Meanwhile I would like to record my thanks to the retiring members of the Board who have helped to bring things to this new stage.

responseSet.title: On Ambiguity, Confusion and the Ego Ideal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Ambiguity, Confusion and the Ego Ideal

Jorge Maldonado

Jorge Maldonado

Jorge Luis Maldonado
Juez Estrada 2725
1425 Buenos Aires

Jorge Maldonado  

SUMMARY

This paper discusses the problem of ambiguity as a form of narcissistic resistance that alters the meaning of words and hinders communication and access to the unconscious. The ego uses ambiguity to avoid and project confusion.

Pathological ambiguity is differentiated from other forms of ambiguity which do not hinder communication. Though they share a common element—oscillation between two opposites—the mechanism involved in this form of ambiguity differs from the mechanism involved in thought processes, ambivalence and metaphor. Two examples are used to investigate the problem of ideals and ego splitting. Ambiguity corresponds to a disorder whereby two contradictory ideals co-exist within the ego ideal. One ideal fixes the discovery of the unconscious as a goal. The other ideal overvalues acting out. The result is that the ego tends to obtain self-esteem through goals that lead to error and it is this quest for error that sustains the feeling of omnipotence.

responseSet.title: Envy and Admiration
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Envy and Admiration

Rolf Sandell

Rolf Sandell

Rolf Sandell
Stockholm County Council Institute of Psychotherapy
Björngårdsgatan 25
S-118 52 Stockholm

Rolf Sandell  

SUMMARY

Envy and admiration are analysed as alternative ways, in essential respects contrary to each other, to cope with situations of relative deprivation, when one becomes aware of having less of some good than some other has. In terms of phantasies and with respect to the organisation of the objects, envy is a paranoid-schizoid phenomenon, with hopelessness, persecutory phantasies, and object-splitting, whereas admiration is in the depressive position, with hope, sympathy, and compartmentalisation of the admired trait out of its whole-object context. The close relations between envy and admiration and narcissism are pointed out, as are the differences between admiration and idealisation.

Freud's Irma Dream

Joseph Bierman M.D.

Joseph Bierman

Joseph S. Bierman, M.D.
2406 Ken Oak Road
Baltimore
MD 21209

Joseph Bierman  

Dear Sirs,

In her article in this journal on Freud's Irma Dream, Barbara Mautner (1991) adduces Freud's use of the word Kalle for bride instead of Braut as part of the line of reasoning for the proof of her hypothesis that 'Freud may have been haunted by the repressed memory of an incident of erotic aggression enacted by himself against his sister Anna when he was 5 years old and she 3 years old' and that 'this experience and its psychic ramifications make up the latent content of the Irma dream' (p. 285). She makes the following point.

July 1992

179

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Homosexualities and the Therapeutic Process

Edited by Charles W. Socarides and Vamik D. Volkan. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1991. Pp. xii + 315.

Christopher Meyers

Christopher Meyers

Christopher D. Meyers
3525 Prytania Street, Suite 518, New Orleans, LA 70115

Christopher Meyers  

This collection of fourteen papers by seventeen contributors expands the perspective of its senior editor, who has long maintained that homosexuality is an analysable phenomenon. Homosexuality is seen as a result of discoverable developmental failures and as a symptom of inner conflict. Consequently, the exploration of the problem in a psychoanalysis can enable a suitable patient to resolve conflicts, resume development, and achieve heterosexual functioning.

Other current opinions dispute this perspective. Some writers hold that homosexuality, though arising initially from distorted concepts of a person's own masculinity or femininity, or from constitutional factors, may be compatible with high-level object relationships and deep commitment to a sexual partner. Thus, treatments which approach homosexuality as pathological may be superfluous or harmful. The authors of this collection would disagree (while stressing the importance of tact and respect for the patient's autonomy), but the book is not explicitly polemical.

Ten papers are organised around one or two cases, considered from the point of view of one aspect of theory or technique. The case descriptions are generally clear and vivid and most of the papers focus on men. Ellen Siegel, however, presents a carefully detailed theory of women homosexuals, with a focus on the evolution of a particular case, and William Meyer and Charles Keith present a female-to-male transsexual. Siegel's and Ira Brenner's papers contain especially valuable material on the analyst's countertransference.

Howard Levine's case shows that homosexual behaviour may serve to regulate self-esteem. Even with a patient who expresses the desire to change his orientation, a preliminary period of analysis devoted to understanding this homeostatic function may be needed before homosexual issues can be directly explored. (Jerome Blackman makes a similar point, but chiefly by elaborating the theory of the instinctualisation of ego functions and ego defects.)

responseSet.title: Personality Death, Object Loss, and the Uncanny
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personality Death, Object Loss, and the Uncanny

Carolyn Feigelson

Carolyn Feigelson

Carolyn Feigelson
544 East 86th Street
New York
NY 10028

Carolyn Feigelson  

SUMMARY

The concept of uncanny anxiety can be distinguished from other clinical entities such as anxiety states, depression, or mourning. It has a special relevance to the impact of 'personality death' in which sudden dramatic alterations in psychological function occur. Clinical examples are presented from cases involving neurological trauma and its impact on the beholder.

The uncanny experience is composed of, first, a perception that the object is familiar and unfamiliar in an intertwined way; and second, that the object is wooden and dead in a way that strains object constancy. Lastly, a sense of a double, of one person within another, develops in the observer.

The uncanny sensation is viewed as a reaction to this doubling. It is a symptom that occurs in reply to the threatened emergence of the unconscious belief that there really are two people there. This belief would be a threat to the ego's soundness. Uncanniness rushes in to protect the ego from feeling attacked by something completely unreal.

responseSet.title: Musical Pleasure
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Musical Pleasure

Norah Gramajo

Norah Gramajo

Norah Nilda Gramajo Galimany
Col. Italia Providencia
44640 Guadalajara
Mexico

Norah Gramajo  

SUMMARY

Music reactivates foetal experiences of pleasure connected with auditory contact with the mother. Subject to an appropriate setting, the porosity of the skin-ego is increased, permitting bidirectional traffic between inside and outside.

Kristeva uses the word significance to denote the meaning occurring along the spectrum from the infralinguistic level (the semiotics of affects) to language. The infralinguistic level (sonic, rhythmic, visual etc. traces) is that on which music develops, music here being presented as the carrier of nebulous 'fantasies' of fusion with the idealised mother of the foetal era. Primitive musical interchanges are illustrated by a clinical vignette.

The paper develops the hypothesis that the pleasure of music revives the primitive auditory fusion with the mother; it points out the relations between aesthetics and musical pleasure and between musical forms and the production of pleasure in the unconscious.

responseSet.title: Response to Maya Slater's Review of H. C. Halberstadt-Freud: Freud, Proust, Perversion and Love
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Maya Slater's Review of H. C. Halberstadt-Freud: Freud, Proust, Perversion and Love

H. Halberstadt-Freud

H. Halberstadt-Freud

H. C. Halberstadt-Freud
Van Breestraat 131
1071 ZL Amsterdam

H. Halberstadt-Freud  

Dear Mr Tuckett,

I am grateful for the perceptive remarks made by the reviewer regarding my thesis on the genesis of perversion. She recognises the central importance of my views on the mother–son relationship typical for perversion: i.e. the illusion of a blissful dyad while the hidden hatred is paired with the exclusion of the father. Thus she endorses my view of the mother of the narrator in Proust's novel as an idealised façade hiding the opposite. This is important because it took long before the riddle of the sphinx of perversion was solved in psychoanalysis, due to the centrality of the oedipal father–son rivalry for Freud. He considered the mother–son relationship as 'the altogether most perfect, the most free from ambivalence of all human relationships'.

While I agree with the reviewer that, as a rule the narrator and the author are not to be confused, in the case of Proust, that turns out to be hardly possible. Proust stated that he wanted to write 'an introspective novel', and he even called the narrator 'Marcel' twice in Remembrance. His letters, moreover, testify to the fact that he felt his mother handled him the way he implied the mother of the narrator did. She, the mother of the author, supervised his behaviour in every respect, criticised him for his relationships, and wanted to control him physically, watching his sleeping hours, his food intake, etc. In short, it would be quite naïve to view Remembrance and Proust's earlier works, like Jean Santeuil, as highly autobiographical in nature.

With regard to the novel as compared to the case history as real life versus fiction, I think the two are not as separate as Maya Slater claims them to be.

As a consequence of my reading of Proust, I take the narrator as a thinly disguised homosexual, although he is never overtly called so by Proust. To this Slater objects. I do not agree that we should abide by the thin veil Proust put on the homosexuality of the narrator. In a discussion with André Gide he stated his aim to hide his homosexuality in his novel.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Development and Psychopathology. Studies in Psychoanalytic Psychiatry

By Clifford Yorke, Stanley Wiseberg and Thomas Freeman. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1989. Pp. 243.

Margret Tonnesmann

Margret Tonnesmann

Margret Tonnesmann
63 New Cavendish Street, London W1M 7RD

Margret Tonnesmann  

In their introduction to Development and Psychopathology the authors refer to the book's history: it was Thomas Freeman who for many years brought to the Anna Freud Centre material from his psychotic patients that he had evaluated with the help of the profile schema of Anna Freud. The case studies were concerned not only with adult pathology but also with the possible childhood antecedents and forerunners of these disorders. In time Anna Freud, Dorothy Burlingham and the authors of this book decided to form a study group with the aim of extending the enquiry to non-psychotic disorders and also of planning publication of the material.

The study group was led by the conviction that a developmental perspective on psychoanalysis is essential for the psychological understanding of an informed psychiatry. The authors stress that they see the value for general psychiatry in the presentation of a psychoanalytic model of the mind rather than presenting psychoanalysis as 'practised in the consulting room' (p. viii). Moreover, they state clearly that they regard psychoanalysis as a method of therapy applicable only to a selected range of disorders but they hope that their presentation will encourage informed psychoanalytic management and treatment of the wide range of disorders this book covers.

In its theoretical orientation the book is firmly based on Freud's metapsychological theories and further developments within the classical psychoanalytical frame of reference. The authors explicitly state that they wish this approach to be distinguished from those psychoanalytic theories of the mind which they regard as far removed from the classical psychoanalytic body of knowledge. Answering the criticism that metapsychology reduces highly organised and differentiated mental activity to the language of forces, energy and structures, the authors stress that metapsychological concepts are of an explanatory nature. They act as tools and are means not ends of our understanding.

The leitmotif of the book is its developmental orientation. The authors regret that early success in linking the clinical presentation of symptoms with pathology based on internal conflict and unconscious processes has encouraged analysts to dismiss the importance of symptoms. They refer to Anna Freud's diagnostic schema which assures a multidimensional diagnosis and assessment of the manifold elements that make up the personality. The developmental factors are organised in the concept of the 'developmental lines'. Phenomenology, psychopathology and nosology are seen as essential for the psychoanalytic understanding of normative and pathological states and the question should be asked 'Why this symptom or that pathology?' Different psychopathology may give rise to the same symptoms and different symptoms may show the same underlying psychopathology (pp. 211-12).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Good Society and the Inner World: Psychoanalysis, Politics and Culture

By Michael Rustin. London and New York: Verso. 1991. Pp. 270.

Jane Temperley

Jane Temperley

Jane Temperley
89 Canfield Gardens, London NW6 3EA

Jane Temperley  

Professor Rustin is a sociologist with an intimate knowledge of psychoanalysis of the Kleinian and British Object Relations school. He is in a specially advantaged position from which to observe psychoanalytic theory and practice in its relation to the contemporary intellectual and political scene. He has the perspective of an outsider but has a profound and sure understanding of the psychoanalytic tradition he is studying. It is a relief and a pleasure to find the psychoanalytic world illuminated and put in context without being misrepresented.

He begins with an examination of whether his commitment to psychoanalysis is relevant to his political position as a socialist. Socialism and Marxism may have failed because they took too narrow an economic view of human needs. Psychoanalysis has and can, he contends, enrich socialism through its understanding of human needs that transcend the merely material.

He is particularly drawn to the object-relations school of British psychoanalysis which stresses our human dependence from birth on the nature of our relationship with the human beings around us, originally our mothers. It is the psychoanalytic tradition which rejects Freud's theory of primary narcissism that appeals to him. Psychoanalytic study of the vital role of the mother in enabling, or failing to enable, the child to cope with his emotional experiences and to develop a capacity to be reflective and to think has for Rustin wider social and political implications. He describes how such awareness has already had social impact in education and in such practices as encouraging parents to accompany small children to hospital.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Un Bouclier Dans L'Economie Des Etats Limites L'Espoir

By Anna Potamianou. Presses Universitaires de France. 1992. Pp. 155. ('Hope: A Shield in the Economics of Borderline States.')

John Steiner

John Steiner

John Steiner
The Tavistock Clinic, 120 Belsize Lane, London NW3

John Steiner  

We are so accustomed to think of hope as a good thing that the reader may be startled, to begin with, by the central idea of this book, namely that hope can function as a type of character armour (a protective shield) which keeps reality at bay and allows the patient's suffering and masochism to flourish. The author addresses the problem of waiting and finds that in both normal and neurotic individuals hope can be a positive influence, sustaining a link with the good object and making waiting more bearable. In borderline patients, however, it can be the very opposite and serve as an expression of the patient's narcissistic self-sufficiency. In these cases waiting is made bearable by recourse to omnipotence, which is sustained by hope. For those patients whose attention is focused on a future which is always 'to come', the present has only secondary importance, so suffering is tolerated in the hope that a due reward will make it all worthwhile. In this way, hope supports and prolongs the masochistic stance; Potamianou describes the various ways it may do this.

Even the reader with only rudimentary French will find something of value in this book. The author is a member of the Greek Psychoanalytic Study Group but trained in Paris and is also a member of the Paris Psychoanalytic Society. The French emphasis is evident throughout the book and makes it suitable for analysts who want to improve their understanding of how our colleagues across the Channel work and think.

In the introduction, the author describes how in normal individuals hope functions as a link with the good object and rests on a self-image stable enough to wait for the object to reappear. However, she convincingly argues that it has its roots in narcissism and the development of a grandiose self that denies the link between the dependent child and the lost object. In the cases of borderline and narcissistic patients it serves to prevent the disintegration of the ego during the period of waiting, but at a cost. Loss is denied, change is prevented and mourning is interfered with.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein: Vol. I, First Discoveries and First System. 1919- 1932. 1990. Pp. 313. Melanie Klein: Vol. II, the Ego and the Good Object. 1932-1960. 1991. Pp. 281

By Jean-Michel Petot. Translated by Christine Trollope. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press.

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Spillius
20 Achilles Road, London NW6 1EA

Elizabeth Spillius  

This is an important but also an irritating book. It is important because it is a major study of the development of Klein's thought. Using his background in philosophy, Petot examines her work to see how it developed as a theoretical system. He shows how her theories evolved from her clinical experience, how she maintained some concepts throughout her work, developed others as she went along, and abandoned others in favour of new concepts she found more adequate. The book is irritating because Petot is so keen to show how Klein makes conceptual distinctions that he sometimes loses the general trend in a maze of conceptual differentiations. This tendency to miss the wood for the trees is much more marked in Volume II than in Volume I.

Volume I makes a useful distinction between what Petot calls the 'Proto-Kleinian System', which is his conceptualisation of the ideas Klein was developing until about 1923, and the 'First Kleinian System', which is his definition of the ideas that Klein evolved as she developed the play technique and began to conceptualise the findings it revealed. In my view, neither the Proto-System nor the First System was really a new theory, for Klein was following the ideas of Freud, Ferenczi, Abraham, and Jones. She was, however, applying their ideas to new material, the findings of the analysis of children; she was discovering new clinical facts, which I think she was not yet able or even trying to integrate into a new theory of her own. Petot carries out a most useful task, however, in teasing out the assumptions and ideas, many of them partly implicit, that Klein was gradually developing during this early period.

Klein's first analytic work was with her son Erich; at first in the form of what Petot calls 'psychoanalytic education', and later in the form of two spells of analysis proper, from 1921 to 1923.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Projective and Introjective Identification and the Use of the Therapist's Self

By Jill Savege Scharff. New Jersey and London: Jason Aronson. 1993. Pp. 340.

Jane Kite

Jane Kite

Jane V. Kite
2489 Washington St., San Francisco, CA 94115

Jane Kite  

Starting with Melanie Klein, theories of projective identification and the related concepts of introjection, incorporation, and identification have had an essential but elusive place in psychoanalytic thinking and clinical practice. Typically, these concepts have been applied almost exclusively to the patient. Jill Scharff's stated aim in this book is to demystify projective and introjective processes in general, and in particular to elucidate their role in the therapist's countertransference. She seeks to clarify the operation of empathy and intuition in the therapist's clinical reasoning, building a theory of therapeutic action that rests on unconscious introjective and projective identificatory processes in the mind of the therapist/analyst. This is a fascinating but tall order, which I believe Dr Scharff fulfils only in part.

The most successful aspect of the book is a superb review of the literature on projective identification, including a lucid tour through the confusions and reversals in definitions and terminology since (and including) Klein. Unlike other authors on the subject, Scharff lauds the excessive conceptual play in the uses of projection and projective identification as essential to the on-going creative potential in a theoretically and clinically useful but essentially ambiguous idea. In going on to develop her argument for the role of projection and introjection in the countertransference, Scharff makes an argument for projective identification as a 'two-body' (intersubjective) phenomenon, adding the comparatively forgotten concept of introjective identification as the bridge to the therapist's countertransference response. Here she tantalisingly suggests that as a profession we have ignored introjective identification in order to avoid recognising ways in which analysts can introjectively identify with their patients. This trenches on the current debates over the role of the analyst's subjectivity in the analytic process, which are alluded to, but not reviewed. Scharff's occasional shifts in frames of reference reveal a tautological dimension in some of her points, including her opinion that our hospitality to the concept of projective identification may depend on how we personally negotiated the paranoidschizoid phase. In the main, however, Scharff's careful exegesis of clinically useful concepts in the first third of the book stands as a welcome addition to the literature on projection and introjection.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fundamentals of Psychoanalytic Technique

By R. Horacio Etchegoyen. London: Karnac Books. 1991. Pp. 863.

Patrick Casement

Patrick Casement

Patrick Casement
122 Mansfield Road, London NW3 2JB

Patrick Casement  

This book, translated from the Spanish, is described in the Foreword by Wallerstein as 'an impressively comprehensive effort to truly encompass the world-wide literature … in the four major psychoanalytic languages … English, German, French, and Spanish' (p. xxviii), and so it is. Etchegoyen shares with us the fruits of his extensive study of all the most significant works on psychoanalytic technique. Wallerstein points out only one serious exception, that of Erikson. One other interesting omission is that of Robert Langs.

In the Preface, Etchegoyen mentions his analytic background: his training analysis with Racker and subsequent analysis with Meltzer; his supervision with Betty Joseph, Money-Kyrle, Grinberg, Herbert Rosenfeld, Resnik, Hanna Segal, Marie Langer, Liberman, Esther Bick and Pichon Rivière. It is, indeed, an impressive list, and it is not surprising that he describes himself as a 'fanatical Kleinian'. He is, in fact, anything but that, being both unusually generous in his consideration of other writers and open in his criticism of some Kleinian excesses. And, throughout, he remains close to clinical experience as the ultimate test of all theory—a recurring theme being his wish to demonstrate the links between theory and practice at every stage, being critical of those who remain too wedded to their theory: 'we analysts sometimes listen more to our theories than to our patients' (p. 549).

The book is pleasingly constructed, each chapter leading naturally to the next. Being a textbook, it is also a great advantage that each new section begins with a summary of the discussion leading to it, so that it can be used as readily for reference as read from cover to cover—a task that was for this reviewer as pleasant as it was long. Much of this pleasure comes from the author's delight in making a logical analysis of all that he presents, comparing one position with another, and in his care in assessing each author within the framework of his/her own theoretical position. And when he introduces an author whose views cannot be appreciated without knowing where that author stands, Etchegoyen provides the reader with the necessary background; as with Lacan, Winnicott, Kohut, and others. It is also a great advantage to English-speaking readers to be introduced to the riches of the Spanish literature on psychoanalysis, most particularly from Racker and Liberman, but many others as well.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freuds Moses-Studie Als Tagtraum [freud's Study of Moses as a Daydream]: Sigmund-Freud-Vorlesungen, Volume 3

By Ilse Grubrich-Simitis. Weinheim: Verlag Internationale Psychoanalyse. 1991. Pp. 116.

Lore Schacht

Lore Schacht

Lore Schacht
Panoramastr 6/2, D-7830 Emmingden 13, Germany

Lore Schacht  

Ilse Grubrich-Simitis has been involved in the editing of Freud's work for many years; her knowledge of his oeuvre is very considerable and she herself contributes assiduously to increasing our own familiarity with it. In this biographical essay, derived from a lecture on Freud delivered at the University of Frankfurt, she has formulated and richly amplified an idea which may at first appear strange to the reader, but which he suddenly finds himself beginning to accept: the idea that the study of Moses can be read as a kind of daydream which arose in traumatic conditions of extreme hardship.

The author describes how this study of Freud's aroused her particular interest and held her attention in thrall, so that she ultimately came to apply Freud's own method to this work in an attempt at psychoanalytic interpretation.

In the course of her extensive study of Freud's surviving manuscripts, she had happened upon Freud's handwritten notes for his book Moses and Monotheism (1939). She had always been interested in that 'first draft' which had already been mentioned by the publishers of the Gesammelte Werke in 1941, which bore the unusual subtitle of The Man Moses, a Historical Novel.

The author's first concern was to determine in what respects the early version differed from that subsequently published by Freud. In the early years of his career, Freud had set no store by the preservation of his manuscripts, but had instead simply thrown them away as soon as the finished printed version was in his hands; this continued to be his practice until 1914. But even then, as Grubrich-Simitis reports, only clean manuscripts free of corrections usually survived—immaculate fair copies of the subsequent printed versions. To her considerable astonishment, the author discovered that the various Moses manuscripts were quite unlike this model, but contrasted with it in their imbalance and unevenness. She graphically compares them to a fissured quarry in the midst of the classical landscape of manuscripts hitherto known to her.

When the Body Speaks: Psychological Meanings in Kinetic Cues

Edited by Selma Kramer & Salman Akhtar. Northvale, NJ and London: Jason Aronson. 1992. Pp. 210.

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies

Alfred Margulies
1493 Cambridge Street, Cambridge 02139

Alfred Margulies  

no mortal can keep a secret. If his lips are silent, he chatters with his fingertips; betrayal oozes out of him at every pore (Freud, 1905, pp. 77-8).

Mahler's work has been so thoroughly taken up in our basic theories that beginner psychotherapists regularly use the term 'rapprochement' without having read Mahler at all. Ironically, general usage often signifies the blunting of hard-won theoretical clarity. Concepts need to be made new again—even strange—if we are to recapture the sharpness of an original insight.

This book is a collection of papers initially presented at the Twenty-Second Annual Margaret Mahler Symposium on Child Development. Mahler's work serves as a prism through which the contributors refract their own conceptualisations, taking as their common theme the nature of nonverbal communication, its sources in early experience, and its relevance to clinical work.

Mahler recognised that child analysts must rely on nonverbal communication more than other analysts; so it is all the more striking that most of the contributors to this volume are not, in fact, child clinicians. This allows the reader a fresh chance to explore the more general applicability of Mahlerian conceptions to nonverbal phenomena in adult clinical work, to return again to the problems Mahler posed, and to reassess her ideas and their appropriation into our theoretical canon.

The book is organised around four main presentations, interspersed with four discussions that stand as chapters in their own right. The effect is to immerse the reader in material that is turned slowly over in the mind: we can re-read, savour, and argue in the imagination. It is impressive how the authors work and re-work the phenomenon of nonverbal communication through the concepts of separation, individuation, and rapprochement.

The introductory chapter by Selma Kramer, 'Nonverbal manifestations of unresolved separation-individuation in adult psychopathology', sets the context of the book. In this chapter we have the invaluable perspective and summation of an expert who has lived with Mahlerian theory and appreciated its power and edges. The essentials of a Mahlerian developmental framework are outlined and reviewed: symbiosis, differentiation, practising, rapprochement and its significance for self and object constancy. In straightforward language, Kramer provides summaries, clear implications of theory, and contrasts these ideas with other conceptual approaches to self and object (e.g. the 'conflicted' Mahler infant v. the 'happy' Kohut baby). This chapter is helpful for beginners, but more experienced practitioners will appreciate the lucidity of thought and the illustrative clinical material.

responseSet.title: Thomas Theodore Steiger Hayley (1913-1993)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Thomas Theodore Steiger Hayley (1913-1993)

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

David Tuckett
63 New Cavendish Street
London W1M 7RD

David Tuckett  

Tom Hayley, Editor of the International Journal from 1978-1988 and Joint Editor from 1988 until his death in June this year, became a psychoanalyst after a career as a social anthropologist and officer in the Indian Civil Service. His was a life of generosity, energy, hard work, caring and, above all, service.

He was born Thomas Theodore Steiger in Ceylon, the son of a Swiss father and an English mother. As was the custom then, he was sent back to England to a boarding Public School. The limitations of sea travel meant that he did not see his parents for a long time. He was first at Clifton College Preparatory School and then later at the Senior School. He was head boy and captain at rugby football and other games at both schools, before going to Peterhouse College, Cambridge, where he was to gain first-class honours in Social Anthropology. He was a scholar of the College and awarded the Leafe Research and Bartle Freer Exhibition. On leaving Cambridge he left for Africa to conduct social anthropological field research for a year among the Lango tribe in Uganda, eventually publishing a monograph, The Anatomy of Lango Religion and Groups (1947), published by Cambridge University Press.

After he and his father became naturalised British citizens, he changed from his father's German name to his English mother's maiden name, Hayley; he was eligible to enter British Government service—something he achieved by gaining entry into the Indian Civil Service in 1937. His induction training involved a probationary year at Exeter College, Oxford, during which he learned about relevant aspects of Indian law and language and horse-riding, before making the sea voyage to arrive in Assam in 1938. For the next few years he was Under-Secretary and then Director of Publicity and performed his duties in such an exemplary and widely appreciated way that after Independence in 1947 he was one of only a handful of British Civil Servants to stay on. He was then Director of Rural Development and Secretary to the Government of Assam (a country with a geographical area the size of England and Wales) and directly responsible to the Prime Minister. He wrote up aspects of this experience for a Ph.D thesis, awarded by the London School of Economics, on 'Ritual Pollution and Social Structure in Hindu Assam'.

responseSet.title: Ángel Garma (1904-1993)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ángel Garma (1904-1993)

R. Etchegoyen

R. Etchegoyen

R. Horacio Etchegoyen
Posadas 1580 13a
1112 Buenos Aires

R. Etchegoyen  

The death of Ángel Garma on 29 January in his eighty-ninth year profoundly saddened the psychoanalytic community in Argentina and throughout the world. A man of great eminence, one of the pioneers of psychoanalysis, a master and a fighter, Ángel Garma was a firm believer in psychoanalysis and liberty. He brought psychoanalysis to Buenos Aires and subsequently played a major role in its dissemination throughout Latin America, by tenacious exertions over many years, in which, assisted by other, no less outstanding men, he displayed great lucidity, single-mindedness and unflagging effort.

He remained as active as ever until a few years ago when the symptoms of the Parkinson's disease which had been gradually undermining his body and nervous system worsened. His mind, however, remained clear until the end, and his spirit remained indomitable, overcoming moments of intense depression with the help and devotion of Elizabeth Goode Rasmussen, his wife and noble companion, and with the support of his children and the entire extended family, which included not only blood relations but also his friends and followers and ultimately a group of analysts scattered throughout the world.

Although dismayed by the progress of his illness, we could not but admire his lust for life and the courage he displayed in the face of his growing limitations, calmly and obstinately determined as he was not to yield to adversity and disability. He stood until his very last day, although not with deliberate intent, as an exemplar of his own theories, of the untiring struggle of the life instinct against the mute and fateful workings of the death instinct; symbolically, death came to him as evening fell on the last Friday in January, when the Buenos Aires psychoanalysts were just beginning their summer holidays. It was as if he had wanted to stay with us until the very end of our working year.

In his wheelchair, he attended the First Argentine Congress of Psychoanalysis, held in the General San Martín Theatre in Buenos Aires in November 1988, and he took a full part in the proceedings of the Buenos Aires Congress in 1991, the 37th IPAC, which was, among many other things, virtually a celebration in his honour; unfortunately, that other great pioneer, Celes Ernesto Cárcamo (1903-1990), was not there, having died a year earlier, on 7 April.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

'Father, Don't you See I'm Burning?' Reflections on Sex, Narcissism, Symbolism, and Murder: From Everything to Nothing

By Leonard Shengold. New Haven/London: Yale University Press. 1991. Pp. 185 + xiii.

Alexandra Harrison

Alexandra Harrison

Alexandra M. Harrison
183 Brattle St., Cambridge, MA 02138

Alexandra Harrison  

Shengold's book is not written for readers who are looking for a guide to psychoanalytic theory or technique, nor for those who desire an exploration of a particular psychoanalytic concept in detail or in historical context. But for readers who wish to think deeply about the narcissistic dilemma of 'all or nothing' throughout the life cycle, with a particular emphasis on the later stages of life, it is a compelling and satisfying book. The book will appeal primarily to psychoanalysts, but any reader who enjoys thinking about psychoanalytic themes in literary works will find this book fascinating.

The book is as profound and provocative as its title. The title comes from a dream told to Freud by a patient, and refers to the child's sexual excitement as he presents it to his parent. Yet the line from the dream has multilayered meaning; it collapses intense emotions and impulses channelled through the body into dream imagery, the discourse of psychoanalysis, and the search for self-fulfilment. The book describes a journey—a journey from the womb to the grave, from omnipotence to deterioration, from everything to nothing. This journey is always accompanied by sex, murder, and catastrophic loss, and those who travel it successfully emerge at the journey's end with the knowledge of having 'something'.

Shengold writes from the perspective of a psychoanalyst of adults. He illustrates the universal narcissistic struggle to reconcile everything and nothing with clinical examples remarkable for their rich use of unconscious symbolism. One feature of Shengold's writing that distinguishes him from other psychoanalytic writers is his discussion of the savage parts of human nature, such as murderous rage, cannibalism, and incest. Shengold's capacity for accepting and understanding this part of the human experience provides a model for the practising psychoanalyst. His examples, both clinical and literary, also bring these feelings and impulses forward so that their major role in the narcissistic dilemma can be fully appreciated.

[[RunningHead]]

The Adaptive Design of the Human Psyche. Psychoanalysis, Evolutionary Biology, and the Therapeutic Process

By Malcolm O. Slavin & Daniel Kriegman. New York: Guilford Press. 1992. Pp. 336.

Jacob Jacobson

Jacob Jacobson

Jacob G. Jacobson
1636 16th Street, Boulder, Colorado 80302

Jacob Jacobson  

Countering the movement within psychoanalysis away from biology (as a reductionist, dehumanising remnant of the physicalist science of Freud's era), the authors of this book embark on an inquiry they characterise as unabashedly biological, 'explicitly grounded in Darwinian principles' (p. 2).

In this volume, whose ambitious scope is revealed in its sub-title, they endeavour to explore how an understanding of our archaic heritage can be relevant to depth psychology and therefore to our clinical work. They argue cogently that all psychoanalytic theories, even those which are supposedly non-biological, make at least silent, implicit assumptions of universal and invariant underlying structures that function as givens for their viewpoint (Freud's frankly biologically-based id concept, for example, or Kohut's pointedly non -biologically based principle of self organisation). The authors argue that each of the traditions of psychoanalytic thought has grasped its own piece of a complex and comprehensive ground-plan of human adaptive functioning, and tends to appropriate those segments of evolutionary theory that seem to bolster it. They believe the full development of this evolutionary ground-plan can replace these implicit, incomplete, and exploitative uses of evolutionary theory, and provide a basis for selecting and synthesising the truths offered by each of the traditions.

Early in the book the authors present their definition of such psychological deep structures, in a statement that also represents their credo: 'adaptive psychological deep structures—like the self, or introjects—are those universla, shared ways of processing experience that have been favored by "natural selection". That is, they have been shaped and retained as human capacities because, over millions of years, they allowed individuals to negotiate more successfully (though often unconsciously) the incredible complexities of the human social environment' (p. 10).

Embedded in that statement is a strict adaptationist view, which, while sometimes illuminating, is at other times unconvincing, and presents some problems despite the authors' efforts to mitigate them. For one thing, this view is a subject of considerable argument among thoughtful, knowledgeable students of evolution. For example, Lewis Thomas dismisses as 'highly improbable' (p. 164) the hypothesis that the obvious Darwinian advantage of the capacity for speech explains its survival and spread. Similarly, Stephen Jay Gould, certainly a staunch Darwinian, views pervasive adaptationism, while interesting and evocative, as 'hyper-Darwinian reductionism' (p. 47). While the authors themselves acknowledge objections of that kind by a number of prominent evolutionary thinkers as 'important caveats' (p. 58), they nevertheless present this view, at the least prematurely, as the modern evolutionary outlook.

[[RunningHead]]

The Doctor, the Patient and the Group: Bálint Re-Visited

By Enid Balint, Michael Courtenay, Andrew Elder, Sally Hull & Paul Julian. London: Routledge. 1993. Pp. 162.

Kenneth Sanders

Kenneth Sanders

Kenneth Sanders
20 Basing Hill, London NW11 8TH

Kenneth Sanders  

When the National Health Service came to post-war Britain, general practice was at a low ebb. The family doctors thought that inferior status had been allocated to them, compared with their colleagues in the hospitals.

A pronouncement that general practitioners were

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

1296

doctors who had fallen off the ladder of success was particularly galling. It was a gaffe by a medical Peer, a member of the very Establishment that had failed to teach its students that patients had minds as well as brains.

How frustrating to find the waiting-room full of hypochondriacs with nothing wrong with them!—nothing, that is, that could be detected by the equipment provided at medical school. Relief came from the Tavistock Clinic in London, where Michael and Enid Balint offered psychoanalytical work group seminars for GPs. They succeeded brilliantly in attracting doctors willing to make an attempt to understand the problems that were on their patients' minds.

Michael Balint's book reporting on this work was widely studied both at home and abroad. A groundswell of approval confirmed that the Balints understood the perplexity of doctors faced with so many baffling psychological and psychosomatic phenomena. The style was invigorating, enthusiasm was great, confidence began to return. There came into being a Balint Society and an International Balint Society with its own journal.

The present volume reassesses progress almost half a century later, during which a reversal has taken place—hospital doctors contemplate budgetary cuts and closed wards while general practice flourishes: 'Formal training for general practice is statutory. Academic departments of general practice are country wide. General practice literature is burgeoning. Research is fashionable. The health service is undergoing radical change. Where does the spirit of Balint blow?' (p. 93).

This is an account, including transcripts of tapes, of a Balint group of a specialised kind, a research group recruited mostly from doctors with Balint work experience associated in one way or another with academic departments of general practice.

responseSet.title: Truth and Reality in Psychoanalytic Discourse
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Truth and Reality in Psychoanalytic Discourse

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber  

Dear Sir:

Thank you for this opportunity to respond to Dr Hamilton's letter.

On her further consideration of my work, Dr Hamilton notes that there appears to be an inconsistency in my position, that I mix a relativistic with an objectivistic terminology, which leads to a certain confusion in portraying my clinical stance. It is, however, my view that what may seem an inconsistency, speaks rather to a central paradox: the analyst's participation is intrinsic to the patient's experience, but our interest in that participation, and in our interaction, resides with how it is experienced—however hidden or defended—by the patient. There is a truth we seek to discover, not to construct—and it is that of the patient. That is our data base, our investigative terrain. It is surely difficult to tease apart 'theoretical perspective' from 'observed data', 'inference' from 'discovery', but this is precisely the process in which I believe we must stay engaged. Though we may not fully attain it, we must continuously strive towards it. To do so will require our sharpened attention to the nuances in the patient's stated and unstated communications, which in turn, can inform us of just how we may have clouded these distinctions, and indeed, have confused 'outward appearance'—as it looks to us—with 'inward experience'—as it feels to the patient. Sustaining this position will further necessitate a vigorous effort to keep clear the delineation between our vantage point and that of the patient, to recognize that our 'truth' is only that—our own, and to distinguish hypothesis from evidence. (See Schwaber, Book Review of Psychoanalytic Case Studies, edited by Sholevar & Glenn, in this Journal, 1993, pp. 409-413, in which I comment on the frequency with which these boundaries are blurred.)

Dr Hamilton writes as though implying that I am posing yet another model, namely, 'the listening stance'. That is not my intent. It is rather, to suggest some considerations about how we listen no matter what our theoretical preference, which my stir further reflection on what we mean by reality when we are speaking of experienced reality. But it is surely the case that any perspective—perhaps even one about how we listen—risks becoming reified, a phenomenon against which we might stand guard.
