[[RunningHead]]

Conflict and Compromise: Therapeutic Implications

Edited by Scott Dowling. Workshop Series of the American Psychoanalytic Association, Monograph 7. Madison, Connecticut: Int. Univ. Press. 1991. Pp. 238.

Jeffrey Nason

Jeffrey Nason

Jeffrey D. Nason
293 Franklin Street, Newton, Massachusetts 02158

Jeffrey Nason  

This is a volume which manages to be both absorbing and disappointing. Clearly intended as an exposition of the ideas of Charles Brenner and Jacob Arlow, it suggests a purpose persuasive and polemical rather than exploratory. Apparently straightforward in its opening premises (Arlow writes: 'Psychoanalysis is first and foremost a psychology of conflict', p. 3), its principal contributors elaborate Brenner's idea of the centrality to psychoanalysis of compromise formation, as, in Dale Boesky's words, 'not only our best explanation for pathogenesis … (but) also our most advantageous theory to account for clinical improvement' (p. 29). Along the way it becomes clear that their argument is with those who stress the importance of deficit in many, if not most, patients and who are dissatisfied with the implications of conflict and compromise theory for therapeutic technique.

I have tried to imagine the impression this book would produce on the reader unfamiliar with recent controversies in psychoanalysis, perhaps a curious but psychoanalytically unsophisticated scholar from a neighbouring academic field. Such a reader would probably find it difficult to decipher what this controversy is all about. All psychoanalysts, and certainly all the contributors to this book, readily acknowledge the clinical demonstrability of deficits in affect tolerance, object relatedness, and cognitive and perceptual reliability in our patients, and all accept the centrality of conflict and compromise formation in neurosogenesis.

Where then does the controversy lie? The answer involves the question of what is primary in pathogenesis. Although this may seem to be a mere matter of emphasis, its implications for the theory and technique of psychoanalytic therapy are far from trivial. Writers such as Arlow and Boesky suggest that the analyst's therapeutic attention to trauma, in the sense of the role of reality in pathogenesis, or to resultant functional deficits in the patient, can distract from optimal analytic attention to underlying intrapsychic conflict. For them it is conflict which ultimately determines both the meaning and force of trauma and the resultant form and content of apparent deficit.

Translating Freud

Edited by Darius Gray Ornston, Jr. New Haven and London: Yale University Press. 1992. Pp. 251 = xix..

Stanley Coen

Stanley Coen

Stanley J. Coen
390 West End Avenue, New York, NY 10024

Stanley Coen  

I highly recommend this book to a psychoanalytic audience on two counts. Firstly, for those who have not already sampled some of the recent writing on problems of reading Freud in translation, this book is an excellent guide. The reader will be impressed that subjective bias must contribute to every reading and every translation of Freud. He will learn how our 'Freud' has been shaped by multiple forces of taste, perspective, and even political organisational pressures which have influenced how we translate and read Freud. English-speaking readers will perhaps need most to consider how the Standard Edition has affected our grasp of Freud's psychology, although Ornston's volume makes clear that the Standard Edition has influenced translation of Freud into every other language.

This book makes the reader want to grasp Freud more immediately, vividly and humanly, by diminishing the barriers of formal structure and reification. All the contributors evoke a Freud who seeks to engage his reader passionately with multiple techniques of fiction and of psychoanalysis. The reader of Freud also becomes an analysand whose resistance is to be interpreted and pushed aside by the force and persuasion of Freud's rhetoric. Ornston's volume thus deals not only with translation but with contemporary problems of reading and of being a reader, of Freud, as of every author. This is my second reason for recommending Ornston's book. Here, problems of translation and reading intersect as the contributors explore the force-field between the author, his work, and the reader.

Two themes in particular emerge from recent writing on translation. Firstly, most students of translation now emphasise the translator's act of interpretation during the act of translating. That is, close study of the choices made by the translator in rendering a text into the target language show that the translator has his own agenda, often not even fully known to himself, for influencing the reader. The more fully the translator can acknowledge such motivation, the less he will be driven unconsciously by such wishes to persuade the reader that the translator now offers the key to unveiling the true reading of the author's text.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamic Functions of the Act of Reading

Vivian Dent and Stephen Seligman

Stephen Seligman

Vivian Dent
Dept. of Psychiatry Mount Zion Medical Center University of California
San Francisco CA 94120
Stephen Seligman
SAN FRANCISCO

Stephen Seligman  

SUMMARY

This study of 68 avid fiction readers demonstrated that for those who love fiction, deeply personal meanings pervade the act of becoming absorbed in a work and shape the ways that they can use fiction in their lives as a whole. Each respondent had a unique, largely unconscious relationship to the act of reading, a relationship that expressed themes and addressed functions related to self-organisation, real and fantasised relatedness, affect regulation, and the repetition or working through of conflict. To the extent that these inner determinants of the reading experience became rigid and unyielding, readers derived less newness, richness, and depth, though not necessarily less benefit, from engagement in fiction. Greater flexibility allowed reading to take place in what Winnicott called potential space. It

1265

enabled readers to involve themselves in fiction without sacrificing either the work's uniqueness or their own inner life, so permitting them to create new experience.

The Chamber of Maiden Thought: Literary Origins of the Psychoanalytic Model of the Mind

By Meg Harris Williams and Margot Waddell. London and New York: Tavistock/Routledge. 1991 Pp 217.

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows

Kate Barrows
8 Hughenden Road, Clifton, Bristol BS8 2TT

Kate Barrows  

The title of this book is taken from a passage by Keats:

Imperceptibly impelled by the awakening of the thinking principle—within us—we … get into the Chamber of Maiden-Thought … intoxicated with the light and the atmosphere, we see nothing but pleasant wonders, and think of delaying there for ever in delight. However among the effects this breathing is father of is that tremendous one of sharpening one's vision into the heart and nature of Man—of convincing one's nerves that the World is full of Misery and Heartbreak, Pain, Sickness and oppression—whereby This Chamber of Maiden-Thought becomes gradually darken'd and at the same time on all sides of it many doors are set open—but all dark—all leading to dark passages—We see not the balance of good and evil. We are in a Mist—We are now in that state—We feel 'the burden of the Mystery' (Keats, letter to Reynolds, 3 May 1818).

The 'Chamber of Maiden Thought' is a metaphor returned to repeatedly by the authors in their attempt to show how English literature has foreshadowed psychoanalytic attempts to understand the creative processes of the mind. The book attempts to trace the evolution of the ideas of Shakespeare, Milton, Blake, Wordsworth, Coleridge, Keats, Emily Brontë and George Eliot. The main thesis is that literature has found ways of describing conflicting or unconscious feelings and of containing these feelings in such a way that emotional development can take place in a central character or characters. Literary works are seen to have anticipated many of the insights of psychoanalytic thinking. The 'Chamber of Maiden Thought' seems to represent a place in the mind where not knowing can be tolerated and where there is an intense aesthetic experience as well as increased awareness of dark or painful feelings.

There is a departure in this book from the tradition in psychoanalytic literary criticism of writing a character-analysis of the hero (cf. Jones). While this is still seen to be of major importance, the literary works are looked at from many other points of view, including the structure of the work, the role of the other characters, and the social or historical setting. In particular, attention is paid to writers' views on the nature of art and of creative processes, as manifested in their works as well as in their writings about art. For instance the authors say of A Midsummer Night's Dream

responseSet.title: The Misuse of Persons: By Stanley J. Coen. Hillsdale, New Jersey and London:  The Analytic Press.  1992.  Pp. 330.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Misuse of Persons

By Stanley J. Coen. Hillsdale, New Jersey and London: The Analytic Press. 1992. Pp. 330.

Barry Landau

Barry Landau

Barry J. Landau
5225 Connecticut Ave, N.W. Washington, DC 20015

Barry Landau  

This book is an interesting and valuable integration of Dr Coen's clinical experience and a review of the psychoanalytic literature. It is rich in material and potentially controversial ideas. Among its many strengths is the author's careful delineation of the specific patient population on whom this study is focused. These patients initially appear analysable and may function well in life outside analysis, yet have difficulty using the analytic method. Coen defines the syndrome as 'pathologic dependency', which involves the 'misuse of others', i.e. the use of relationships with other people to serve the patient's own defensive need to feel safe and not alone and to avoid feeling responsible for themselves and for their intrapsychic conflicts. Because these patients are so frightened of the rage and destructiveness within themselves, they seek complete submission and attachment to their object, which is idealised and perceived as omnipotent. They use the analytic situation, including the analyst's interpretations, not to gain insight and to free themselves, but rather to maintain the pathologically dependent tie to the analyst. Therefore, their analyses become stalemated.

Coen presents richly detailed, convincing illustrations of how analysing the defensive use of passivity and helplessness to obscure unconscious rage and hostility can help these patients to achieve autonomy and competence. In addition, his discussion of the importance, and the difficulty, of analysts' tolerating the patients' hostility, is pertinent and closely reasoned. Further, his portrayal of patients who have a sense of defect and victimisation demonstrates effectively the crucial role of conflict theory in understanding and helping the patients, by analysing the uses they unconsciously make of their handicaps and hardships. At the same time, the emphasis on the defensive and regressive elements distinguishes Coen's idea of 'pathologic dependency' from deficit-model concepts, such as Kohut's 'self-selfobject pathology'. Another area of interest is Coen's detailed exploration of the relationship among 'pathologic dependency', perversion, and 'perverse object relations', including his explication of the defensive uses of 'sexualisation' and repetition.

responseSet.title: Tangled Patterns: Freud, Vienna, and the Brain
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Tangled Patterns: Freud, Vienna, and the Brain

Valerie Greenberg

Valerie Greenberg

Valerie D. Greenberg
Dept. Of German and Slavic Languages
305 Newcomb Hall
Tulane University
New Orleans
LA 70118

Valerie Greenberg  

SUMMARY

A pattern that prevails in Freud's early texts and carries over into his life suggests a culturally-derived coherence in Freud's thinking and work. This pattern connects the early studies in neuro-anatomy, the description in Studies on Hysteria of the structure of hysterical neuroses, urban design in Vienna, antisemitism, and the role of bourgeois values in Freud's life. The theoretical basis for this view depends upon the work of cognitive psychologists who define thinking as pattern recognition. I propose that consideration of such features as the structure of physical space can lend additional dimensions to our understanding of Freud, and that a complex appreciation might include attention to recent interdisciplinary developments such as chaos theory, and lead to a redefinition of the notion of 'cultural context'.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Madeleine Baranger

Madeleine Baranger

Madeleine Baranger
Sevilla 2954
1425 Buenos Aires

Madeleine Baranger  

Madame Janice de Saussure begins by laying stress on the psychoanalytic concepts which are the premises of our work. I did not explicitly mention these in my paper, perhaps because I somewhat rashly assumed that they were taken for granted and shared by all analysts.

I agree with her that the search for the unconscious is what dominates the analyst's mental attitude from listening to interpretation.

This attitude is one of the defining elements of psychoanalysis and distinguishes it from all other types of psychotherapy. Both de Saussure and I have realised in our teaching of psychoanalysis that the most difficult thing to achieve with a candidate—and indeed with any patient—is for him genuinely to accept the existence of the unconscious.

In the search for the unconscious we encounter difficulties and resistances—foremost among which is its own forgetting—which are manifested in the field of the transference, apart from the fact that, as Freud pointed out, in the dialectic between the pathogenic process and the process of therapeutic recovery 'the battles are not fought in the same place'—that is to say, the repetition in the transference is always indirect, and only a part of the pathogenic history is manifested in the transference relationship.

The other premise is that the essence of the analytic procedure consists in 'making the unconscious conscious', which means that conscious understanding must give rise to changes in the patient's experience and behaviour. De Saussure emphasises the relativity of this understanding and warns us not to mistake interpretation for a magic word (we may misunderstand and forget). If the analyst fails with his verbal magic, he may become disillusioned with his specific tools and resort to various expedients and techniques which will eventually take him outside the framework of psychoanalysis. As de Saussure points out, we

Translated by Philip Slotkin.

(MS. received October 1993)

responseSet.title: Two Discussions of 'Theory in Vivo' by Dennis Duncan
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Discussions of 'Theory in Vivo' by Dennis Duncan

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer

Roy Schafer
241 Central Park West #1A
New York, NY 10024

Roy Schafer  

I very much appreciated Dr Duncan's frank, engaging, open-minded account of his way of proceeding 'from listening to interpretation'. I certainly agree with him that today's psychoanalytic world is increasingly pluralistic and that, accordingly, analysts now have an opportunity either to choose from among a variety of modes of listening and intervening, or to try to find ways to combine their best features. I also agree that these modes originate not only in different systems of psychoanalytic thought and theoretical contributions of original thinkers, but also in the retained personal belief-systems which have been the raw material that our educational experiences have more or less shaped. The latter point deserves emphasis.

Dr Duncan's tone is consistently celebratory, and not without reason; however, there is also a dark side to pluralism. This dark side includes incompatible understandings of clinical material, irreconcilable differences of method and interpretation, and the frequent undecidability of questions concerning analytic progress. For the sake of balance, the following discussion is offered to articulate this other side of recent historical developments.

The best place for me to begin this job, I have decided, is with Dr Duncan's illustrative clinical account, for as I read it I soon realised that I was witnessing an analytic scene and hearing an analytic dialogue rather different from the one he had presented. Before turning to some theoretical considerations of the burdens imposed by pluralism, and in order to ground those considerations in concrete experience—'in vivo' to use Dr Duncan's phrase—I want to review in detail my alternative reading of his material.

Dr Duncan's report begins with Mrs Brown's row with her husband, a man she describes as having 'charming little ways', a gang of admiring male friends, an inflated sense of importance, a tendency to use silence punitively, and an inclination to deny conflict—all of which she sees as covering significant depression, insecurity, and anger. At this point, I began to think that Mrs Brown might have been obliquely presenting a severe critique of Dr Duncan and not, as he thought, provoking him by telling a bland, one-sided story that implies envy of her husband and perhaps of him, the analyst, as well. This thought stems from my practice of trying to hear real or imagined countertransference implications in whatever analysands bring up, especially in their opening statements; this practice helps me define where the transference is at that moment and also serves as a form of supervision of my own countertransference.

responseSet.title: Beneath the Analytic Surface: The Analysand's Theory of Mind
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Beneath the Analytic Surface: The Analysand's Theory of Mind

Donald Spence

Donald Spence

Donald P. Spence
Dept. of Psychiatry Robert Wood Johnson Medical School
Piscataway, N.J., 08854

Donald Spence  

SUMMARY

A growing body of research on the way children change their forms of thinking and views of the world from around the ages of 2 to 5 strongly suggests that something called a 'theory of mind' is acquired during this time which significantly affects the course of language acquisition, moral development, interactive problem-solving and other cognitive attributes. As we learn more about the adult varieties of this theory of mind we begin to realise their implications for psychoanalysis. This paper considers such issues as: what kind of theory of mind is assumed by our reliance on the Basic Rule? What kind of theory is

736

required to understand the usual range of standard interventions? How can deficiencies in the analysand's theory of mind interfere with standard interpretations? As research on theory of mind makes us increasingly sensitive to the importance of different forms of thinking about thinking, we can apply many of these insights to common problems of psychoanalytic technique.

responseSet.title: Teaching and Learning in an Analytic Mode—A Model for Studying Psychoanalysis at University
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Teaching and Learning in an Analytic Mode—A Model for Studying Psychoanalysis at University

Rivka Eifermann

Rivka Eifermann

Rivka R. Eifermann
Department of Psychology The Hebrew University
Mount Scopus
Jerusalem 91095

Rivka Eifermann  

SUMMARY

The neglected issue of teaching psychoanalysis to non-practitioners, specifically in the university setting, is addressed and a model is put forward, which recognises the many difficulties that are inevitable to such an undertaking. Evolved in the course of two decades of teaching psychoanalysis at the university, the model is based on the recognition of certain essential parallels, alongside evident differences, between the teaching and learning of psychoanalysis and the analytic process itself. As in analysis, the inevitable difficulties encountered in the teaching–learning process are treated in this model as integral to the process rather than as interfering with it. The analyst teaching psychoanalysis in accord with the model uses his/her skills to create an atmosphere in which the analytic stance is pervasive, which encourages the student to learn in an experientially-near way. Major features of the model are discussed, and ways of examining, evaluating and refining it put forward.

<autaff><addr><ln>Marion Minerbo</ln><ln>Rua Caçapava, 49/144</ln><ln>São Paulo, S. P.</ln><ln>CEP 01408-010</ln><ln>Brasil</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Some Comments on Baranger's Paper Using Jacobs's Clinical Example
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Comments on Baranger's Paper Using Jacobs's Clinical Example

Marion Minerbo

Marion Minerbo

Marion Minerbo
Rua Caçapava, 49/144
São Paulo, S. P.
CEP 01408-010
Brasil

Marion Minerbo  

This excellent article by Madeleine Baranger is both consistent and didactic. She provides the reader with an abstraction of the steps which take place in the analyst's mind between what he or she hears and the interpretation. She thus manages to come near the clinical work of each one us. She theorises on the precise nature of the analyst's work, both when in silence and when interpreting. To give more life to Baranger's theoretical considerations, I have chosen to let Jacobs's splendid clinical material explain and illustrate the points that I have chosen.

WHAT THE ANALYST LISTENS TO

The first significant aspect of Jacobs's article is that, as he recounts the session, he gives us some indications as to who this patient has been during analysis. To do this he makes reference to his own feelings and sensations even before the patient has entered his consulting room. These points are valuable because they throw a very interesting light on our first question: what the analyst listens to.

In a 1961 article, W. & M. Baranger propose that the analyst's listening focuses on a structure they call the intersubjective field. They present this concept as a solution to an epistemological problem regarding the nature of the unconscious, a question which I will not take up here.

What is the intersubjective field? It is a structure that goes beyond the intersubjective analyst–patient relationship and even beyond the transference–countertransference interaction. It is what produces both; it produces the analytic dialogue itself. This structure represents a new Gestalt. It is the focus of the work of interpretation (1961).

The intersubjective field, therefore, represents more than the sum of the two parts involved, just as a melody is much more than the sum of the notes that make it up. 'It is not simply a question of taking the analyst's countertransferential experiences into account, but of recognising that both this countertransference and the patient's transferential manifestations have the same origin: a basic unconscious phantasy that, as a creation of the field, has its roots in the unconscious of each of the participants'.

I appreciate Dr Bierman's commentary, and I agree it is likely that Freud heard this story in Yiddish. The critical point, however, is whether he was aware of the German slang usage. responseSet.title: The Personal Myth in Psychoanalytic Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Personal Myth in Psychoanalytic Theory

Barnaby Barratt

Barnaby Barratt

Barnaby B. Barratt
7480 Greenwich East, Bloomfield, Michigan 48301-3920

Barnaby Barratt  

Edited by Peter Hartocollis and Ian Davidson Graham. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1991. Pp. xviii + 413.

Remembering the Personal Past: Descriptions of Autobiographical Memory. By Bruce M. Ross. New York and Oxford: Oxford University Press. 1991. Pp. xii + 244.

It is a pleasure to review two such different books, both of which belong in every psychoanalyst's library and address issues at the heart of psychoanalytic discourse: how the past constitutes the personal present, and how the present personal organises itself by reference to its own past, inscribed and reinscribed at the margins of literality and figurativity. As I shall suggest, such matters also strike at the core of our resistances to psychoanalytic discourse by attesting to the instabilities of all self-knowledge and the impossibilities of epistemological unity or totalisation.

The Personal Myth in Psychoanalytic Theory is a substantial anthology resulting from the First Delphi International Psychoanalytic Symposium, cochaired by Peter Hartocollis and Ian Graham, which brought together 250 participants and over 30 extensive papers from a variety of psychoanalytic viewpoints (structural-functional, self-psychological, object-relational, post-Kleinian, and independent). This volume includes five major papers (by Jacob Arlow, Otto Kernberg, Donald Meltzer, André Green, and Ernest Wolf); ten other original papers, which are either commentative or elaborative (clinical and applied); a reprinting of Anna Potamianou's interesting 1985 paper; a syncretic discussion by Robert Wallerstein; as well as a preface, an introduction and an epilogue by the editors.

It was Graham's idea that the topic of 'personal myth' could serve as a 'bridging concept' around which diverse theoretical orientations could be related. This inspiration has proved to be both brilliant, in that this is a rewarding collection of papers, and quixotic, to the extent that the volume illustrates rather poignantly the ways in which our divergent theories are and are not intertranslatable. None of the contributors herein wants to dispute Ernst Kris's pioneering 1956 essay, but several want to extend it in profligate directions. Thus, it seems generally agreed that every personal myth defends against consciousness of impulses, expresses unconscious fantasies, and is to various degrees adaptive or maladaptive; but what is perhaps surprising is how little this consensus does to regulate differences of terminology, articulated viewpoints, and underlying assumptions. Perhaps this is because these papers exemplify a common failing in contemporary psychoanalytic literature, namely that the wealth of clinical study is unconstrained by a commensurable capacity for philosophically-informed self-criticism. Almost every contribution passes too lightly over the philosophical issues it invokes—issues that are inherent to the cogency of this topic—and this limits both the explication of its own suppositions and the elucidation of genuine debate. The two major issues, that are, I believe, encircled too warily for our own good here concern the relation between the mythic and what is non-mythic (or at least putatively so), and the relation between the particular and the general (or the individual and the collective).

London:  Karnac Books.  1992.  Pp. 280.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><aut authindexid="Rosen, Maria" listed="true" role="author"><nfirst>Maria</nfirst><nlast>Rosen</nlast></aut>

responseSet.title: The Making of Maleness—Men, Women and the Flight of Daedalus: By Peter Tatham.  London:  Karnac Books.  1992.  Pp. 280.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Making of Maleness—Men, Women and the Flight of Daedalus

By Peter Tatham. London: Karnac Books. 1992. Pp. 280.

Maria Rosen

Maria Rosen

Maria Rosen
40 Priory Gardens, London, N6

Maria Rosen  

In this original book the author suggests the story of Daedalus as an archetypal model and pattern for maleness in our epoch. Tatham contends that the model provided by the Daedalus story has been largely ignored until now, to the detriment of the concept of maleness in our time, and argues forcefully

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1993

650

and persuasively for its inclusion. He further states that the emphasis he sees placed on the hero as a model has obscured the potential value of the Daedalus model.

The author carefully describes the course of Daedalus in his life's journey; showing his ability to survive, live a long life and bring to fruition different creative aspects of himself. He shows that, by the end of his life, Daedalus has made very significant cultural contributions. This happens especially by virtue of his quality that the author describes as 'well-craftedness'—which includes dedication to skill and craftsmanship, endurance, determination, imagination and the ability to use imaginative flight to create inventions and constructions.

Imaginative flight is an important aspect of the escape of Daedalus and his son, Icarus, from their confinement in the labyrinth where Minos has incarcerated them. The author explores the mythical figures and background in which Daedalus moved and lived. He demonstrates Daedalus's relationships to figures in his life and how these relationships bring out different aspects of him and contribute to his psychological journey and development.

Tatham also describes the story of Daedalus and his nephew Talus, unfolding its meaning at a psychological level and using it to illustrate another aspect of psychological development. The author's delineation of this is skilled and masterful (epithets he himself would apply to Daedalus). Exploring meaning is undertaken in the traditional Jungian way of understanding myth as parallelling process in psychological development.

The author clearly holds the view that development of the soul may require the renunciation, or 'death', of certain desires at points in life. This is depicted in his treatment of the story of Daedalus and Talus in an ingenious and yet very lucid way. It is also depicted in the treatment of the Daedalus/Icarus story, in which the youth Icarus gives in to his urge for total abandonment to non-directed flight, with the ultimate consequences.

[[RunningHead]]

Gender and Psychoanalytic Treatment

Edited by Morton Kissen. Current Issues in Psychoanalytic Practice. Monographs of the Society for Psychoanalytic Training, No. 5. New York: Brunner/Mazel. 1992. Pp. 102.

Lora Tessman

Lora Tessman

Lora Heims Tessman
62 Kirkstall Road, Newton, Massachusetts 02160

Lora Tessman  

The dynamics of gender in psychoanalytic treatment have sparked the concern of psychoanalysts ever since Freud invoked the concept of transference to contend with the fervent declarations of love by female patients toward their male analyst. He noted that this phenomenon 'may be evaluated from two points of view, that of the doctor who is carrying out the analysis and that of the patient who is in need of it' (Freud, 1915, Observations on transference-love. S. E. 12, p. 160).

Editor Morton Kissen's Introduction to Gender and Psychoanalytic Treament leads one to anticipate further insights about the current fate of the 'evaluation' from these 'two points'. However, the book's seven contributors devote themselves more clearly to specifying developmental obstacles to gender identity—also an interesting topic—than to the gendered interaction between patient and analyst. Margaret Foerster, in 'Freud and gender: the script as artifact', critiques Freud's 'deficiency theory of the acquisition of femininity'. 'The struggle for gender differentiation in boys' by Elaine Siegel details boys' 'early castration anxiety' preventing identification with their fathers. Anni Bergman, in 'The mother's wish for a better self: mutual identification between mothers and daughters', highlights 'the effect of the mother-child relationship during separation-individuation' 'on the child's identity themes' and illustrates this with lovely case material. In 'Gender and super-ego development' Morton Kissen's 'central thesis' is 'that normal super-ego structuralization' is 'directly linked to the palatability of the same-sex parent as an identification figure'. Dale Ortmeyer, in 'Idealisation, gender and the psychoanalyst', argues that culturally-shaped gender ideals unconsciously constrict both patient and analyst, unless the analyst differentiates 'ideal and real' selves. Suzanne B. Phillips, on the 'Influences of gender in the analysis of the homosexual', stresses the unconscious meaning of the gender of the analyst chosen by the homosexual patient. In 'Gender related barriers to intimacy', Judith Davis reflects on a contemporary population of 'emotionally aloof' career women with a counterdependent overvaluation of autonomy who are like 'traditionally socialized men'. The final chapter, by editor Kissen, 'A variant of the "Whore-Madonna" complex in contemporary women', refers to Freud's description of the 'Whore-Madonna split in the love relationships of men', and points to the pre-oedipal determinants of a 'fairly similar paradigm' in women.

[[RunningHead]]

Female Identity Conflict in Clinical Practice

By Doris Bernstein. Edited by Norbert Freedman & Betsy Ditsler. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1993. Pp. 197.

Nadine Levinson

Nadine Levinson

Nadine A. Levinson
30131 Town Center Drive, Ste. 216, Laguna Niguel, CA 92677

Nadine Levinson  

The late Doris Bernstein was a talented and original psychoanalytic thinker, a sensitive clinician, and a revered teacher of both psychoanalytic theory and process. The editors, Norbert Freedman and Betsy Ditsler, have done an admirable job integrating her life work for this IPTAR Monograph.

Written over the last thirty years, this collection of essays reflects Bernstein's commitment to the concept of a gender-specific view of the clinical process. Her writings are contemporary; we encounter a language of object relations, a rhetoric about the evolution of the self, as well as a discourse of something 'wonderfully of old'. What is old is Bernstein's emphasis on the importance of uniquely-female body experiences for the formation of female identity.

Bernstein systematically examines the regressive effects of early female genital sensations and their impact on female character, superego formation, and the mastery of adult modes of functioning. She does so by using impressive clinical examples of women, and she reveals her genius by weaving together a revitalised meaning in the links between bodily sensations and instincts, psychic reality, and the impact of the relationship with both the mother and father on psychic outcomes, gender configurations, and sexual identity. Unquestionably, Bernstein's expositions stand in contrast to most contemporary psychoanalytic writings, which underrepresent the instincts and denude the mind of the vital role of the body.

The theoretical framework and organisation of the book is summarised in Chapter 1, 'Gender-specific attribution of identity', co-authored by Bernstein & Freedman, wherein the developmental, structural, and object-relations threads contributing to the central thesis, of the pervasiveness of gender on every aspect of mental functioning, are sketched. The book is organised more by theoretical cogency than by chronology. The developmental considerations are expressed in everyday language in Chapter 2, followed by her updated seminal exposition on genital anxiety in Chapter 3. In Chapters 4 and 5, Bernstein takes a different perspective from Freud in looking at the superego and Oedipus complex in females. Chapters 6 and 7 feature the manifestations of her theories in the clinical process and their depiction in fairy tales and mythology.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Electrified Tightrope

By Michael Eigen. Edited by Adam Phillips. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1993. Pp. 289 + xxviii.

Steven Goldberg

Steven Goldberg

Steven H. Goldberg
2116 Sutter Street, San Francisco, CA 94115

Steven Goldberg  

'From the outset it seemed clear that I could never be a strictly orthodox analyst, whatever that may mean. I loved Freud and Jung, body therapies, phenomenological and existential philosophers, Gestalt psychology, Melanie Klein, D. W. Winnicott, and the British Independents, R. D. Laing and Harold Searles, and later, Kohut, Lacan, and Bion' (p. 261). So writes Michael Eigen in the Afterward to this collection of twenty of his previously published psychoanalytic essays. It is not surprising, then, that these papers explore a distinctive and unusual range of interests not often explored in the psychoanalytic literature.

Though five of these papers have appeared in the pages of this journal, the remainder have appeared in journals and books less likely to have come to the attention of most analysts. Accordingly, the publication of these papers in one volume provides a valuable service to the reader interested in Eigen's work, which also includes two psychoanalytic books, The Psychotic Core(1986) and Coming Through the Whirlwind(1992). The Introductory Notes and Afterward by the author, along with the excellent Introduction by the editor, are useful and orienting, and assist the reader in negotiating these unconventional papers. Rather than attempt to summarise all of these papers, I will discuss those that, to me, represent Eigen's work at its most original and thought-provoking.

In 'Abstinence and the schizoid ego', Eigen describes a number of patients who chose to undergo periods of profound abstinence and withdrawal in the service of their personal development. These patients, according to the author, withdrew from the outer world in an effort to consolidate a core sense of self, previously suspected but not fully known; this consolidation in turn led to a renewed and deeper connection with the world and an ability 'to grant this deeper sense of I to others' (p. 5).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Technique and Practice of Psychoanalysis, Volume II: a Memorial Volume to Ralph R. Greenson

Memorial Series of the Ralph R. Greenson Memorial Library of the San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Monograph 1. Edited by Alan Sugarman, Robert A. Nemiroff and Daniel P. Greenson. Madison, CT: International Universities Press. 1992. Pp. 500.

Boyd Burris

Boyd Burris

Boyd L. Burris
4545-42nd Street, N. W., Suite 310, Washington, DC 20016

Boyd Burris  

In the first lines of the Introduction to his The Technique and Practice of Psychoanalysis, Volume I (1967, New York: Int. Univ. Press), Ralph R. Greenson spelled out that the time had come for a textbook on psychoanalytic technique. He mentioned years (including Freud), yet he found them lacking in sufficient descriptive details about what is involved in analysing a patient. Greenson recognised that his textbook would not eliminate all the disagreements about technical matters, but hoped it would act as a useful reference by describing in great detail how he went about analysing his patients' psychic phenomena. He had not followed his original plan of utilising a chronological format (Initial Interviews, Transition to the Couch, the First Analytic Hours, etc.) and finally elected to publish an outline of the basic concepts of psychoanalytic theory and technique (Transference, Resistance, etc.) as Volume I of a two-volume work, the second to be organised along the lines of the original chronological concept.

Volume II has now been realised, and in its Preface Daniel P. Greenson (Ralph Greenson's son, an accomplished analyst himself, and one of the editors) tells us how his father struggled to finish Volume I and, in fact, completed it in part because of the prospect of a Volume II. His deteriorating health eventually forced him to abandon Volume II; he had already prepared an Introduction to it, however, which is included in the current volume. The donation of Greenson's professional library to the San Diego Psychoanalytic Institute in 1985 by his widow led to the discovery of the outline for Greenson's intended second volume (four detailed pages) and several unfinished chapters.

In creating this first monograph of the Ralph R. Greenson Memorial Library of the San Diego Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, the editors (Drs Sugarman and Nemiroff are on the San Diego Institute faculty) originally planned to commemorate Greenson by publishing his outline, four of his intended chapters, and contributions from colleagues about topics Greenson meant to cover in Volume II. Although the editors eventually revised this plan, allowing the analytic contributors (mostly training and supervising analysts from American psychoanalytic institutes) to vary their topics from the original outline, this final product of twenty-two papers, each in chapter form, represents a general adherence to the original Greenson format.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How Psychiatrists Look at Aging

Edited by George H. Pollock. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press. 1992. Pp. 232.

Stanley Shapiro

Stanley Shapiro

Stanley H. Shapiro
255 South Seventeenth St., Philadelphia, PA 19103

Stanley Shapiro  

Following coronary by-pass surgery entailing considerable medical scrutiny and stimulating much introspection and self-analysis, the editor evolved the idea of asking selected colleagues 'to prepare what I now call "sectorial autobiographies", with the focus on their own ageing processes' (p. xi). Sixteen essays were received in response to this appeal for baseline data both about growing old personally and how this impacted on their work with elderly patients. It was hoped that professionals would be able to use their own self-observational powers to shed light on this important, neglected area. Though specific questions designed as guidelines were provided, the results vary widely as to the degree of adherence to the assigned task as well as the style and focus in which the material is presented.

Some, such as the first essay jointly written by the now deceased Sam Atkin and his son Adam, are poignantly revealing. Father and son document the struggles of an analyst in his late eighties against the advances of Parkinsonism. It consists of stories interspersed with actual journal entries, in which the former reveal astute, secondary process functioning and the latter, by contrast, reveal more regressed, fantasy-laden, affectively-charged commentary even on what he has written in a scientific mode. Another analyst chronicles his enforced retirement and his attempts to cope with the narcissistic blow and sense of loss encountered, for which his efforts at self-analysis, though valiant, did not suffice.

The essays tended to be of interest in the degree to which they were personal in content, dealing with actual experiences and feelings. At the other end of the continuum were several didactic, over-intellectualised attempts to be scientific as if for publication in a gerontological review, and thus almost devoid of any personal observations. Several of the respondents took the opportunity to update their curriculum vitae, presenting a chronological account of their career development and its epiphanies. Although highly self-referential they seemed to miss the point of the exercise, as if the charge was to provide an account of an exemplary life to serve as inspiration. This monograph would have been better served if these contributions had been omitted.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analytic Experience

By Neville Symington. London: Free Association Books. 1986. Pp. 347.

Eric Rayner

Eric Rayner

Eric Rayner
43 Heath Hurst Road, London NW3 2RU

Eric Rayner  

This is a book of stature and dignity. First published some time ago and now well into its third reprinting, it deserves careful thought. It arose directly out of Symington's lectures to a wide body of students of psychoanalytic psychotherapy at the Tavistock Clinic in London. As he says, the book was forged between him and successive audiences over a good many years. Therefore, the first way we must evaluate the book is as an elementary introduction to psychoanalytic theory and practice. However, the author interweaves a second function with this—illustrating his descriptions of other people's core psychoanalytic theories with his own case material. By serving other's ideas, he slowly unfolds his own philosophy about the therapeutic vocation, and it is this in particular that makes it a book of distinction. There are thus two readerships for this book; firstly, for students, and we will come to this in a moment. Secondly, it could be of use to any analyst or therapist. For instance, I suspect that when even experienced teachers of psychoanalysis read this book with a view to putting it on a reading list, they will find themselves engrossed and absorbed into re-thinking their own philosophy. Let us evaluate this work both as a textbook and as a portrayal of one analyst's technique and therapeutic philosophy.

Symington is a psychoanalyst working in Australia who learnt his trade in London in what is known as the Independent tradition. Here the reader must be warned that the reviewer comes from the same background and finds it easier to agree with Symington's philosophy than many other analysts might.

His first chapter betrays his earlier philosophical and theological training—where most of us would flounder he goes straight to the question of truth. In fact, he entitles the chapter 'Psychoanalysis: a servant of truth'. Here is a flavour of his thinking: he says that truth is real, but it is not of the order of reality that can be touched or heard, nor is it of the sort that has extension. However, most psychological realities, dreams, beliefs and thoughts are not of these sorts of reality either. Truth, Symington thinks, 'lies between'—between two persons seeking it, or between disciplines. It can only be seen or glimpsed, never possessed: 'Someone who claims "I have the truth" has lost it'.

[[RunningHead]]

Being a Character: Psychoanalysis and Self-Experience

By Christopher Bollas. New York: Hill and Wang. London: Routledge. 1992. Pp. 294.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman
The New York Hospital—Cornell Medical Center
525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021

Aaron Esman  

As it approaches both its second century and a new millennium, psychoanalysis appears to be in the throes of a sea-change. Under the shaping influences of object-relations theories, developmental research (Emde, 1990) and newer perspectives in the philosophy of science, the psychoanalyst is, more and more, being seen not as an objective 'neutral' observer but as an engaged participant in the analytic dialogue, and his own affective responses and associations, as much as those of the analysand, are seen as the essential 'material' for the analytic work. Long the point of view of the British 'Independent' group, this approach has become increasingly influential in American psychoanalysis as well, exemplified by the writings of Schwaber, Renik, Jacobs, Stolorow and others.

Christopher Bollas has been a particularly articulate and influential spokesman for this 'new wave' in psychoanalytic thought. In earlier work, he forcefully addressed the importance of what he called the 'unthought known'—i.e. the intenalised preverbal interactions with care-givers, always unconsciously active but inaccessible to waking consciousness—capable of being experientially inferred by a receptive analyst in the course of the analytic process. As intellectual heir to Donald Winnicott, he is also concerned to show the powerful impact of these pre-oedipal, precognitive experiences on a variety of cultural formations that Winnicott would have categorised as 'transitional phenomena'.

Particularly in the first half of the book, Bollas seeks to explore the ways in which we choose the 'objects'—both human and inanimate—that populate our lives and, as he puts it, the ways in which they 'choose' and 'use' us (in this effort he is, of course, pursuing a trail blazed by Winnicott [1969]

responseSet.title: The Analyst and the Mystic: Psychoanalytic Reflections on Religion and Mysticism: By Sudhir Kakar.  Chicago and New Delhi:  University of Chicago Press.  1991.  Pp. 83 + xi.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst and the Mystic: Psychoanalytic Reflections on Religion and Mysticism

By Sudhir Kakar. Chicago and New Delhi: University of Chicago Press. 1991. Pp. 83 + xi.

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

Robert A. Paul
The Graduate Institute of the Liberal Arts Emory University
Atlanta, GA30322

Robert Paul  

This slim volume consists of three essays originally delivered as the Haskell Lectures at the Divinity School of the University of Chicago. The unifying theme of these otherwise unconnected essays is the author's project of juxtaposing a psychoanalytic viewpoint with the mysticism of his native India, not, as he says, 'to "shrink" the mystic, but rather to expand our understanding of his mystery and, ultimately, of the working of our own selves' (p. ix). The distinctive flavour of the book as a whole arises from the conflict between the author's dual loyalties as analyst and as spokesman for the validity of the mystical experience and tradition.

The first essay is also the most substantive and intriguing, presenting rich material, conveyed with grace and lucidity, from the biography of the great nineteenth-century Indian mystic Ramakrishna. Kakar solves his dilemma of wanting to be psychological about his subject without reducing, 'shrinking', or pathologising him, by proposing that relational rather than classical theoretical models are the appropriate ones in the study of someone for whom, as Kakar asserts, 'there is no evidence … which is remotely suggestive of any strong hostility toward the Oedipal Father' (p. 25). To his credit, Kakar provides enough data about his subject, even in the short compass of this 30-page essay, for the analytic reader who might be sceptical about this claim to reach his or her own conclusions about Ramakrishna's psychodynamics.

Of central importance in Kakar's view of Ramakrishna is the latter's purportedly unconflictual identification as a female; diagnosable, as Kakar says, in Stoller's terms as secondary transsexuality. But this is also to be understood in the context of a society in which such phenomena are viewed in a quite different light than is the case among ourselves. In the end, Kakar relies on Winnicott's ideas about femininity as aligned with being, while masculinity is concerned with doing; in this sense, mysticism expresses the unconflictual, primary pre-oedipal feminine element underlying the masculine active-passive model with which the classical model has concerned itself. Indian religious life and experience has allowed itself room for the culturally validated realisation of this dimension of psychic life, while Western monotheism—the context of the classical model itself—has not, thus leaving it with only pathological language in which to conceptualise personalities like Ramakrishna.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Violent Attachments

By J. Reid Meloy. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson Inc. 1992. Pp. 365+xvii.

Henry Krystal

Henry Krystal

Henry Krystal
26011 Evergreen Road, Suite 206, Southfield, MI 48076

Henry Krystal  

Today's local paper carries a big headline: When Rehabilitation Fails. This disturbing headline is followed by two pages of discussion of a murder which took place at the W. J. Maxey Training School. The alleged killer, a 19-year-old man, had been admitted to the juvenile equivalent of a maximum-security prison two years before, after being tried as an adult and found guilty of a knife-point rape of a 36-year-old woman in Detroit. The school handles the young men in groups, trying to motivate the group to lend support to the effort, on the part of the inmates, to suppress the memories of their own previous victimisation, and to support each other in learning to control their aggression. In fact, last summer, when another 'disturbed student' had attacked a teacher, and held a 'sharpened point of a wooden ruler' to her throat, the present offender had been the leader of the expected response—where the whole group grabbed the attacker, and held him down until he calmed down and surrendered. This time, however, he escaped the usual supervision twice on the same day: on the first occasion stealing the key to the social-workers' lounge, and on the second entering the lounge where he allegedly killed, by strangulation, Barbara Synnestvedt, a 46-year-old counsellor.

This incident illustrates the difficulties we have in working out a comprehensive view of the principles both of sheer aggression and of sadistic sexual attacks. For, while most murder cases involve a known person, some murders, rapes, and even relations with prostitutes, depend on the 'strangeness' of the victim—actually a disidentification with the object.

From a psychological point of view, it is more likely that we will gain useful insights from studying the great majority of homicides and various sadistic acts, which are committed on known objects, and the author decides to take this tack. He does make one early point, which we need to keep in mind for a number of reasons: that adult murders involve, in 75 per cent of cases, known and 'close' objects, but that 'homicide deaths of infants are three times greater than toddlers … and that infanticide tended to be as high or higher then the rate of adult homicide' (p. xiii). This point reminds us that the issue of child-sacrifice is still active in our time, and that it must be kept in mind in dealing with a variety of patients. Remarkably, virtually all infanticide is perpetrated by women. Equally challenging is the observation that in polytheistic religions, and world-wide mythology, it was most likely to be a mother-goddess who demanded child sacrifice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contexts of Being: The Intersubjective Foundations of Psychological Life

By Robert D. Stolorow & George E. Atwood. Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press. 1992. Pp. 145+xii.

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert Galatzer-Levy

Robert M. Galatzer-Levy
180 North Michigan Avenue, Chicago, Illinois 60601

Robert Galatzer-Levy  

This short, clear book critiques traditional psychoanalytic thought and offers an alternative. It recommends understanding development and psychoanalysis from an intersubjective position and applies this view to clinical problems. In the intersubjective view, psychological difficulties arise through failures in the interaction of the subjectivities of child and caretaker; these problems may be resolved as they re-enter intersubjective exchange in analysis. The authors believe the analyst has no greater knowledge about reality than the patient, and view the idea of the analyst's authority as both epistemologically and practically unsound. Rather than detecting and correcting the patient's supposed misperceptions of internal and external reality, the analyst's job is to comprehend how the patient constructs subjectivity. The analyst should remain aware that both the patient's construction and the analyst's understanding are profoundly affected by their shared experiences.

Minds, in the authors' view, can never be well understood in isolation but only in interaction with other subjectivities. Psychoanalysis provides an ideal chance to study intersubjectivity. This does not mean that the analyst should merely accept the patient's ideas at face value: the intersubjective point of view includes the idea of unconscious psychological function. It enriches this idea by emphasising how matters that are never brought into dialogue are unavailable for exploration and how fear of loss of needed relationships may lead to forcing matters from awareness.

Stolorow & Atwood describe the implications of their viewpoint for several clinical issues; these include the concretisation of psychological states as bodily experience, the nature of trauma, the therapeutic alliance and therapeutic impasse. For example, they state that 'negative therapeutic reactions' and other untoward developments in analysis are not best understood as arising out of the patient's isolated need to enact past experiences. Nor are they best comprehended as resulting simply from countertransference, although the analyst's attention to his contribution to the impasse is important. It is the joint activity of analyst and patient that produces the impasse. Patient and analyst can resolve these impasses through recognising and working through it as a problem of two interacting subjectivities.

responseSet.title: Freud and his Critics: By Paul Robinson.  Berkeley:  University of California Press.  1993.  Pp. 281.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud and his Critics

By Paul Robinson. Berkeley: University of California Press. 1993. Pp. 281.

Gerald Fogel

Gerald Fogel

Gerald I. Fogel
7 East 81st Street, New York, NY 10028

Gerald Fogel  

In Freud and his Critics, Paul Robinson, a Professor of History at Stanford University, takes on three of Freud's smartest, most effective, and well-known critics: Frank Sulloway, Jeffrey Masson, and Adolf Grünbaum. He also successfully takes them apart, in my view. Robinson accomplishes this not-so-simple task with comprehensiveness, clarity, and brilliance. Anyone who values Freud and his contributions will find the book an immensely satisfying (and possibly self-affirming) read.

This book is no tract; it is impressively fact-filled, and all students of Freud—beginner, intermediate, and advanced—will learn and profit from it. In separate chapters, Robinson goes directly to the home ground of each critic, taking on the arguments of each—detail by detail, point by point. With Sulloway and Grünbaum in particular, his meticulous description of their positions will afford many readers a greater clarity than can be derived from reading the critic's original text. The evidence Robinson marshals from Freud's work in support of his own positions is extraordinarily persuasive—overwhelming in its precision and attention to detail. In addition, his considerable scholarliness, erudition, and wit are combined in a deceptively simple and readable prose style.

Robinson is also far more than a purveyor of 'facts', of so-called hard data—that sort of potential for reductionism is shown to be a characteristic of each of these critics. One might say that in this respect Robinson is quintessentially psychoanalytic, Freudian. He actually humanises these adversaries, infers where each may be coming from. He is not tendentious, but rather is patient, compassionate. After all, one must first appreciate a man—even a critic!—before it is possible truly to understand him. Each of these critics is found to have selected the Freudian facts needed to serve his own purposes, which are multiple and complexly interrelated in every instance. Their motives may be ideological, political, or personal, but Robinson's methodology requires that all arguments be eventually returned to their context, and reductionism and hidden agendas be brought into the open. Common sense must also be served, not merely 'facts' and 'arguments'.

responseSet.title: Psychic Conflict and the External World in Freud's Theory of the Instinctual Drives in Light of his Adherence to Darwin
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Conflict and the External World in Freud's Theory of the Instinctual Drives in Light of his Adherence to Darwin

Jonathan Dunn

Jonathan Dunn

Jonathan Dunn
2919 Sacramento Street
San Francisco
CA 94115

Jonathan Dunn  

SUMMARY

Freud's theory of the instinctual drives is examined in the light of his adherence to Darwinian thought. In this scheme, the drives arise from the interaction between biological and environmental stimuli and are dialectically constructed from both pressures: internal and external phenomena reciprocally organise each other and attempts to conceptualise them as separate entities restrict the degree to which we can understand the nature of mental life. Inhibition that became built-in to biological stimuli redirected and 'elevated' these bodily urges to representational functioning, a phylogenetic

238

process which developed from environmental demands in the species' struggle to survive. These ideas bear upon Freud's notion that ubiquitous mental conflict, environmental sensitivities, and primitive id and ego (including defence) organisations are inherent components of instinctual activity. This construction is compared to Freud's other models of the instinctual drives. The attitude conveyed by the metapsychological suppositions put forward is related to the clinical situation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Freud Klein Controversies

Riccardo Steiner Ph.D.

Riccardo Steiner

Riccardo Steiner, Ph.D. (Psychol)
12A Belsize Lane
London NW3

Riccardo Steiner  

Dear Sir,

I wonder if I could correct some of the statements made by Dr Gillespie in his very nice review of The Freud Klein Controversies (1941-45), published by Tavistock and the Institute of Psycho-Analysis in 1990, and edited by Pearl King and myself. The review was published in Volume 73, Part 1 of this journal on pages 161-64.

On page 164 Dr Gillespie writes 'The only adverse criticism of this book refers to proofreading. There are too many misprints, though hardly any in the editorial contributions; I suspect therefore the bulk of the book was copied without editing from the archival documents'.

I completely agree with Dr Gillespie's criticism and I would add that it is overall in the text of the Scientific Discussions that one can find the misprints. I would like to stress that as editor of the section concerning the Scientific Discussions I made it quite clear to those concerned with the actual publication of the book that I would only proof-read my Editorial Comments and the passages from Freud's Standard Edition which I added to the Discussions. I do not think it worthwhile to explain the various reasons which led me to take this position.

My requirement was accepted by the publishers.

Dr Gillespie would also have preferred that I should have quoted the original German text rather than that of the Standard Edition, because of the problems related to Strachey's translation. While I understand the phiological scruples of Dr Gillespie, I would like to remind the reader that during the Scientific Discussions Freud was quoted in English except in a few minor cases where the discussion tried to clarify the meaning of some expressions which had not been properly translated.

To quote all the passages in German would have created enormous additional editorial problems and, as Dr Gillespie knows, even the original German text of Freud has its own problems.

One should not forget also that only a limited number of psychoanalysts, if one excepts the German-speaking Societies, can read and understand Freud in German today.

responseSet.title: Deficiency and Envy: Some Factors Impacting the Analytic Mind from Listening to Interpretation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Deficiency and Envy: Some Factors Impacting the Analytic Mind from Listening to Interpretation

Judith Mitrani

Judith Mitrani

Judith L. Mitrani
9735 Wilshire Blvd. #422
Beverly Hills, CA 90212

Judith Mitrani  

SUMMARY

This paper examines some aspects of an unconscious process developing within the mind of the analyst; a process affecting both the way in which the analyst listens to the patient's material and the interpretations subsequently formulated. The paper highlights the problem of the analyst's 'mis-use' of theory as a protection against unbearable feelings of psychic pain, evoked by what is perhaps the most essential tool of psychoanalysis: the analyst's emotional contact with the patient's early experiences in infancy.

Both the 'deficiency theory' and the concept of 'primary envy' are called into question during this inquiry. Some of the most recent work of the 'post'-Kleinians (from Bion through to the present-day workers in England as well as in the Americas) is reviewed. Clinical examples, both from the author's own experience and from previously published material, are considered as illustrative of some factors which may impact upon the mind of the psychoanalyst from listening and interpretation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inner Experiences of the Analyst: Their Contribution to the Analytic Process

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore Jacobs

Theodore J. Jacobs
170 East 77th Street
New York
NY 10021

Theodore Jacobs  

SUMMARY

As an illustration of the inner experiences of the analyst as they operate in the analytic situation, this paper presents a single analytic hour in some detail. By means of this clinical material, the author seeks to demonstrate how the thoughts, fantasies, memories, bodily movements and autonomic responses that he experienced in this hour affected his interventions, the kinds of transference-countertransference intereactions that developed in the session, and the unfolding of the analytic process.

responseSet.title: Latency—A Reappraisal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Latency—A Reappraisal

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen
51 Limerston Street
London SW10 0BL

Alicia Etchegoyen  

SUMMARY

This paper discusses the specific metapsychology and developmental tasks of the latency period. It offers a Kleinian view of the phase and compares it with other current psychoanalytic theories.

The emphasis is on the importance of ego and superego growth and integration during latency resulting in better contact with both external and internal reality. Character formation, social integration and learning are discussed as the specific developmental tasks of the period.

Freud's original formulation that the latency period is a consequence of the resolution of the Oedipus complex is evaluated in the light of contemporary developments. I draw on the ideas of Klein and others such as Loewald, Burgner, Holder, Tyson to describe it as a process of progressive and more complex elaboration throughout latency, interconnected with superego development. This process of gradual working through is considered to be a fundamental feature of the period. This model of progressive maturation is illustrated with clinical material.

The clinical concept of pseudo-latency is used to distinguish between the developmental achievement represented by the establishment of the latency organisation in contrast to a defensive structure (pseudo-latency) which hides pathology.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Uniqueness of the Patient–Analyst Pair: Approaches for Elucidating the Analyst's Role

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy Kantrowitz

Judy L. Kantrowitz
334 Kent St.
Brookline
MA 02146

Judy Kantrowitz  

SUMMARY

This paper describes ways in which patient–analyst interactions result in a unique creation in each psychoanalytic endeavour. The particular character of the analyst and analysand overlap in a manner that potentially influences

902

both the depth and the limits of the analysis. Analysts, therefore, need to be alert to their impact on the analytic process. Three approaches for elucidating the analyst's role and its effect on the patient are described. Case examples illustrate the impeding effect of unrecognised similarities between patients and analysts. Once the analysts became aware of these similarities, there was a change in their behaviour, resulting in a deepening and expanding of the patients' analytic experiences.

Varieties of Envious Experience

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Spillius

Elizabeth Bott Spillius
20 Achilles Road
London NW6 1EA

Elizabeth Spillius  

SUMMARY

This paper discusses Melanie Klein's use of the concept of envy and some of the criticisms of it. Two ways of experiencing envy, ego-dystonic and impenitent, are described and illustrated in clinical detail. The paper concludes with a description of a model of the relation of giving and receiving, especially of the conscious and unconscious feelings involved in giving and receiving, for I believe that it is in this relation and its internalisation that envy is particularly likely to be aroused, diminished, exacerbated, or perpetuated.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Pregnancy and its Consequences on her Work

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen
51 Limerston Street
London SW10 0BL

Alicia Etchegoyen  

SUMMARY

In this paper I discuss with clinical examples how the analyst's pregnancy may affect the analysis in unique, subtle and at times unexpected ways to which the analyst must be alert. Like any major psychosocial crisis in the life cycle, pregnancy challenges the analyst's ability to hold on to the psychoanalytic attitude. It also confronts her with specific issues (primitive anxieties about the inside of the body, fears of loss of identity, of damage to the foetus etc.) which have to be acknowledged and which require consistent and at times painful working through.

Overall, my experience was that by and large positive emotional growth took place, both in the patients and myself, during the pregnancy. I would suggest that the possible changes were not only due to the pregnancy per se but to the careful analysis of its effects which allowed for the psychoanalytic situation to be maintained.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Narrative Representation, Narrative Enactment, and the Psychoanalytic Construction of History

Humphrey Morris

Humphrey Morris

Humphrey Morris
8 Irving Terrace
Cambridge, MA 02138

Humphrey Morris  

SUMMARY

This paper develops the idea that the fundamental tension between representation and enactment described by psychoanalytic historical theories also determines and limits our theorising attempts themselves. Freud moves toward this recognition in his late work as he brings a challenge to the lifting-of-repression model of historical recovery. One basis of this challenge is that Freud now implicates the analyst, as well as the analysand, in the tension between the capacity of historical narratives to represent the past, and their tendency to enact it. From the perspective of Freud's explicitly dialogic model in 'Constructions in analysis', the task of establishing historical reference in psychoanalysis cannot be separated from the narrative actions of the analyst. These narrative actions would necessarily include manifestations of denial and disavowal that I designate as 'narrative enactments'. My discussion expands upon this linguistic dimension of questions Freud raises about the limits of the psychoanalytic construction of history. Narrative enactments work against an interpretive restoration of historical reference based upon the lifting of repression, but at the same time, seen as an aspect of narrative functioning, they are founding acts upon which our historical constructions rest.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disruptions of the Therapeutic Relationship in Psychoanalysis: A View from Self Psychology

Ernest Wolf

Ernest Wolf

Ernest S. Wolf
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Illinois 60601

Ernest Wolf  

SUMMARY

The progression of the therapeutic process in clinical psychoanalysis is, characteristically, punctuated by a series of breaches in the otherwise collaborative analytic relationship of analyst and analysand. In this paper, these disruptions are conceptualised from the point of view of self psychology. A focus on the subjective aspects of the analytic relationship, i.e. the selfobject experiences in the transference, illuminates the vicissitudes of the emergence, maintenance and transformation of the

686

self. The analytic process proceeds against an unconscious conflict between the fears of repeated injury and the longing for needed selfobject experiences. Disruptions occur when analysands perceive the analyst to be less attuned to their issues and more involved in the analyst's own agenda. Acknowledgement of the analysand's subjective view as a valid experience must precede the interpretation of the relational dynamics before it can effectively lead to a restoration of the process.

responseSet.title: Some Thoughts on the Presentation and Discussion of the Clinical Material of Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Thoughts on the Presentation and Discussion of the Clinical Material of Psychoanalysis

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

David Tuckett
37 Woodsome Road
Dartmouth Park
London NW5 1SA

David Tuckett  

SUMMARY

This paper is intended to stimulate consideration of the problems we face as psychoanalysts in attempting to discuss our basic data: the material from the clinical setting. Some brief comments are made about the importance of this issue for progress in our field, in the context of several attempts by the author to present and discuss process material from psychoanalytical treatment, and also in the light of discussion at psychoanalytical conferences and congresses, such as the Amsterdam sessions devoted to the clinical papers by Jacobs and Duncan.

The question of what is going when psychoanalysts present clinical material to each other and discuss it is approached: firstly, by drawing attention to some features of the context in which discussion of that report takes place; secondly, by considering what it is we are doing when we select what we report of a psychoanalytic session; and, thirdly, by exploring certain inherent features of the psychoanalytic situation itself and their impact on the construction of a report and the response to it of an audience. Taken together, it is argued, these three elements have quite far-reaching implications for how presenter and audience might usefully play their parts in clinical discussion, and on the nature of the culture of enquiry we need to develop if we are to have hopes of building psychoanalytic theory and technique grounded in observations of practice.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Jacob A. Arlow</cs></ln><ln>94 Wildwood Road</ln><ln>Great Neck</ln><ln>New York, NY 11024</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Two Discussions of 'The Mind of the Analyst' and a Response from Madeleine Baranger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Two Discussions of 'The Mind of the Analyst' and a Response from Madeleine Baranger

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow

Jacob A. Arlow
94 Wildwood Road
Great Neck
New York, NY 11024

Jacob Arlow  

A more timely topic for this Congress than 'The Mind of the Analyst: From Listening to Interpretation' is hard to imagine. In recent years we have witnessed a growing number of contributions to our literature dealing with the inner experience of the analyst as he listens and as he reaches conclusions concerning the mental processes of his patients. If, in keeping with the topic of a recent international Congress, we are pursuing the search for a common ground, it is here, I believe, that we can find it, in the experiences we share in the standard setting of the psychoanalytic situation. At some time each day, every analyst ponders how he proceeded from listening to interpretation. These issues, indeed, are the very centre of our concerns.

We are grateful, therefore, to Baranger (1993) who, from her long and deep experience, placed at the beginning of her contribution the real, the practical issues that bring analyst and analysand together. The patient comes to treatment for some pressing need. Once he has sought out the services of a psychoanalyst, whether he has said so in words or not, the patient feels that there is something wrong with how his mind works, that something is out of his control, and it must be due to factors in him of which he is not aware. Otherwise, he would not consult a psychoanalyst. Elucidating the influence of unconscious elements on mental functioning is the province of psychoanalysts. The real professional relationship is a constant feature of the analytic situation and, however remote it may seem from the task at hand, at some level the patient's productions are directed toward the therapeutic task.

From the very beginning, the relationship of the two subjects in the experiential field is asymmetrical, and this is as it should be, for they both have different functions to perform. Both analyst and analysand are committed to the goal of helping the patient overcome or at least alleviate his difficulties. This is the abiding framework within which treatment takes place. There is always an unconscious selection of presentation in the analysand's thinking, burdened though it may be by the weight of conflict.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Pregnancy: The Patient, the Analyst, and the Space of the Unknown

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti
37 Woodsome Road
London NW5 1SA

Paola Mariotti  

SUMMARY

In this paper I have discussed some thoughts about the effect of the analyst's pregnancy on her patients, using material from my own experience. In the first part of the paper I have examined some of the issues evoked in the countertransference especially in the first trimester of pregnancy, when, because of my concern about the health of the foetus I was reluctant to inform patients of my state. In the latter part of the paper I have focused on some of my patients' responses as expressed in the transference: in

163

particular on the question of how patients dealt with learning of and thinking about my pregnancy, which involved a threat to the sense of unity, fusion and non-separateness from me that in some cases were of paramount importance. To do this I have discussed at some length clinical material relating to two patients, the first a woman whose own pregnancy followed mine, in an imitative fashion that both denied any difference between us and also allowed her to develop her capacity to be a mother; the second a man who initially understood my pregnancy as rejecting of him, persecuting and guilt-provoking, and saw it as an event either unthinkable and/or to be dealt with omnipotently.

responseSet.title: What Sort of a Thing is a Religion? a View from Object-Relations Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What Sort of a Thing is a Religion? a View from Object-Relations Theory

David Black

David Black

David M. Black
30 Cholmley Gardens
London NW6 1AG

David Black  

SUMMARY

This paper suggests that a religion is not, as Freud proposed, a science-like thing, refutable by evidence. It is a socially constructed and maintained system of internal objects, analogous to those spoken of in psychoanalysis. Like analytic internal objects, religious objects have a heuristic function but no material existence. Unlike analytic objects, they are derived from a definite cultural tradition and are elaborated over time to meet the experience of practitioners. They may be understood to have a function of 'containing' the feelings, thoughts and fantasies arising in individual practitioners, and of making these experiences comprehensible. These objects then enable the believer to speak more truthfully of, and relate more fully to, the larger matrix within which the human world is situated. Finally, the suggestion is made that, as with analytic internal objects, religious objects may best be judged by their long-term effects on the lives of believers.
